institution: High-Performance Metal Structural Materials Research Institute, Shagang School of Iron and Steel, Soochow University, Suzhou, China
key_findings: Mechanical strength initially improves and peaks at a Zn/Mg ratio of 1.30, with a yield strength of 536.93 MPa.

institution: High-Performance Metal Structural Materials Research Institute, Shagang School of Iron and Steel, Soochow University
key_findings: Optimal Zn/Mg ratio of 0.89 offers balance between mechanical strength and corrosion resistance.

key_findings: Excessive Al-Mn phases have a poor effect on properties.
Mechanical properties of double-aged ZTM641-xAl alloys are obviously affected by temperature; properties at 150°C are generally better than at 200°C.
ZTM641-0.2Al alloy has the highest elongation at 150°C; Al element can reduce layer fault energy and improve plasticity.
For as-extruded alloys at 150°C, elongation gradually increases from 59% to 71% as Al content increases; strength first increases then decreases.
ZTM641−2Al alloy has the best strength at 150°C: UTS 171 MPa and YS 140 MPa.
Al-Mn phase is a thermostable high-temperature phase that can significantly improve high temperature strength, but too high Al content leads to large Al-Mn phase size, reducing strength.
notes: Data extracted from multiple sections of the provided paper content. The alloy series is referred to as ZTM641-xAl (x = 0, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4%). Specific composition values (Zn, Mg, Ti base) are not provided in the text. Mechanical property values for ZTM641-2Al at 150°C are explicitly given. Elongation range (59% to 71%) is for as-extruded alloys at 150°C with increasing Al content.

paper_title: Revealing the mechanical and degradation properties of the Zn-Cu-Ti alloy with ununiform heterostructure
institution: Institute of Advanced Wear & Corrosion Resistant and Functional Materials, Jinan University, Guangzhou, China
key_findings: The Zn-2.0Cu-0.1Ti alloy with ununiform heterostructure was successfully fabricated via controlled stir casting and hot extrusion.

Improved strength is achieved without sacrificing ductility, attributed to grain refinement and Orowan looping strengthening mechanisms.

key_findings: Ky is 0.43 MPa m1/2.

key_findings: T4 treatment yields highest tensile strength (558.8 MPa) but lowest elongation (1.7%) due to GP zone cutting strengthening and GB blocking effect.
T6 treatment reduces tensile strength to 544.7 MPa but increases elongation to 2.2% due to transformation of GP zones into uniform spherical η phases.
T6I4 treatment further reduces tensile strength to 536.8 MPa and increases elongation to 2.5% due to formation of elongated η phases and clean grain boundaries.
T6 treatment results in the highest open-circuit potential (-0.63 V), followed by T6I4 (-0.74 V), and T4 (-0.75 V).
notes: Data extracted from Section 1 and Section 12. Mechanical properties correspond to T4 condition. Corrosion potential for T4 condition is provided. Other aging conditions (T6, T6I4) have different values but only T4 is fully extracted per instruction to extract numerical values where specified.

key_findings: Hot-rolled Zn-5Ge has a corrosion rate of 0.2255 mm/y in SBF.

key_findings: The ultimate strengths of four alloys with increasing Zn/Mg ratio were 356 MPa ± 4.7 MPa, 372 MPa ± 5.5 MPa, 388 MPa ± 4.3 MPa, and 394 MPa ± 5.6 MPa, respectively.

Elongation values: Alloy A 17.5% ± 1.3%, Alloy B 17.8% ± 2.3%, Alloy C 19.8% ± 2.1%, Alloy D 18.6% ± 3.3%.
Both ultimate tensile strength and yield strength showed a clear increasing trend as the Zn/Mg ratio rose.
notes: Data extracted from Section 18. Alloy compositions (Zn/Mg ratios) are not explicitly given in wt%, only relative ratios. Mechanical properties are for peak-aged state at ambient temperature.

notes: Data extracted from Section 3 of the provided content. Composition calculated as balance Zn with 25 wt% Sn and 0.09 wt% Al.

key_findings: Superplastic deformation of Zn-0.033 Mg alloy exhibits strong dependence on deformation temperature than strain rate.
Ultrafine-grained Zn-0.033 Mg alloy achieves superplastic elongation of 376 ± 79% at 453 K and 0.001 s−1.
Grain boundary sliding is the dominant mechanism, accommodated by {1012} tensile twins.
notes: Elongation value is 376 ± 79% (mean ± standard deviation). Data extracted from Section 1.

key_findings: Hot compression deformation of Zn-0.5Mg-0.05Fe alloy shows three phases: work hardening, dynamic softening, and steady-state fluidization.
Intrinsic equation for hot compression: ε̇ = 1.334 × 10^10 × [sinh(0.0053σ)]^7.62947 × exp(-109.931691/RT).
Safe processing zones: 25°C–130°C with 0.13s⁻¹-0.36s⁻¹ strain rates, and 120°C–200°C with 0.01s⁻¹-0.6s⁻¹ strain rates.
Both DDRX and CDRX occur during hot deformation.
DRX reduces deformation resistance, refines grains, and improves mechanical properties.
notes: Data extracted from Sections 1, 8, 10, and 12. Composition calculated as balance Zn. Phases inferred from Section 12 (Mg2Zn11 and RE_xZn_y) but RE elements not specified in this alloy; phases listed are typical for similar Zn-Mg based alloys. Mechanical properties not directly provided for this specific alloy in the evidence.

Coexistence of η' and T' phases with high number density contributes to high strength.
Discontinuous distribution of grain boundary precipitates (GBPs) in low Zn/Mg ratio alloys reduces exfoliation corrosion susceptibility.
Increased Zn concentration in AlZnMg phases at higher Zn/Mg ratios deteriorates corrosion resistance.

key_findings: The tensile strength of Al-Zn-Mg alloys is governed by grain boundaries when they become weak zones, with intragranular precipitation remaining less changed.
Mg atoms segregate dramatically at grain boundaries due to water quenching in Al-Zn-Mg alloys.
The Zn/Mg ratio should be reduced, Zn content not too high, and Mg content not too low to achieve high tensile strengths and crushing energy absorption.
A four-stage aging treatment decreased tensile strength and yield strength by only 3% and 4% but increased elongation and impact toughness by 15% and 17%.
The quenching rate more strongly influences precipitates and precipitation free zones than over-aging treatment.
Variations in Zn/Mg ratio modulate solute concentrations and alter precipitation kinetics, impacting mechanical strength and corrosion resistance.
Mn and Zr additions effectively inhibit recrystallization in Al-Zn-Mg alloys.
Increasing Zn/Mg ratio boosts peak hardness and increases ultimate tensile strength and yield strength in Al-Zn-Mg alloys.

paper_title: Effects of Ti on microstructure, mechanical properties and biodegradation behavior of Zn-Cu alloy
institution: Key Laboratory for Microstructure Control of Metallic Materials of Jiangxi Province, Nanchang Hangkong University
key_findings: Zn-2Cu alloy consisted of Zn matrix and CuZn5 phase; TiZn16 phase newly formed with Ti addition.
As Ti content increased, CuZn5 phase was further refined; TiZn16 phase changed from fibrous to discontinuous reticular morphology.

Zn-2Cu-0.05Ti alloy exhibited optimal mechanical properties: yield strength, compressive yield strength, ultimate tensile strength and elongation increased by 37.2%, 52.9%, 38.7% and 19.1% respectively compared with Zn-2Cu.
notes: Based on evidence from Section 8. Specific numerical values for mechanical properties of Zn-2Cu-0.05Ti are not provided in the extracted text, only percentage improvements. Composition is nominal (Zn-2Cu-0.05Ti).

key_findings: After hot-rolling, the grain of Zn-1.5Cu-xAg-0.1Zr alloy was broken and new phase CuZn5 was precipitated.
The mechanical properties were significantly improved after hot-rolling.
Maximum YS, UTS and EL of as-rolled Zn-1.5Cu-1.0Ag-0.1Zr alloy were 218.14 MPa, 244.83 MPa, 64.50% respectively.
notes: Data extracted from Section 3. The specific composition for the reported mechanical properties is Zn-1.5Cu-1.0Ag-0.1Zr. The paper discusses hot-rolling as the processing method.

processing.casting_method: water chilling copper mould ingot metallurgy processing

key_findings: Optimum solution treatment is 470°C/60 min.
Ultimate tensile strength, yield strength and elongation of the aged studied alloys reached a maximum value, 581 MPa, 561 MPa, and 10.1%, respectively.
Both tensile strength and hardness increase first and then decrease with increasing solution temperature or solution time.
notes: Composition from Section 3 (Al-5.48Zn-2.02Mg-0.39Cu-0.28Mn-0.26Sc-0.11Zr). Solution treatment and mechanical properties from Sections 14 and 17 (470°C/60 min, UTS 581 MPa, YS 561 MPa, El 10.1%). Phases from Section 17 (fine Al3(Sc, Zr) particles).

paper_title: The microstructure and mechanical properties of Zn-25Sn-XAl (X=0–0.09 wt%) high temperature lead free solder
institution: National Cheng Kung University
key_findings: Addition of Al up to 0.09 wt% enhanced UTS from 67.28 to 78.61 MPa (16.84% improvement)
Yield strength improved from 42.52 to 52.81 MPa (24.2% improvement)
Elongation decreased from 39.02% to 32.83% with Al addition
Al addition refines grain size and depresses undercooling behavior
Needle-like zinc-rich phase of eutectic region coarsened upon Al addition
notes: Data extracted from Section 2 and Section 3. Strain rate for mechanical testing was 8.3×10^-4 s^-1 (0.00083 s^-1). Room temperature testing assumed as 25°C based on typical conditions.

key_findings: The addition of Al enhanced the undercooling of Zn-25Sn-XAl alloy, promoting nucleation of liquid solder.
ZS-XAl alloys exhibit better mechanical properties than high lead content alloys.
Long-term reliability of Zn-25Sn-XAl alloy may need further investigation due to Al segregation concerns.
notes: Data extracted from Section 1. UTS and elongation values are for as-cast ZS-XAl (X=0.09%) at room temperature with strain rate 8.3e-4 s^-1. Composition: Zn-25Sn-XAl with X=0.09 wt% Al. Based on limited evidence, room temperature assumed 25°C.

paper_title: Precipitation behavior and mechanical properties of Al-Zn-Mg alloy with high Zn concentration
institution: Guilin University of Technology, Yanshan University
key_findings: Mg content affects precipitation behavior and mechanical properties of high Zn-content Al-Zn alloy.
Dynamic precipitation of Zn and η phases occurred during hot rolling.
Zn phase promoted precipitation of η phase, which increased in size with increasing Mg content.
Mg addition led to decomposition of Al-Zn supersaturated solid solution and precipitation of η' phase during natural aging at room temperature.
notes: Based on Section 1 of the provided paper content. Specific composition values (wt%) and numerical mechanical properties are not given in the extracted text.

processing.casting_method: high vacuum high pressure die casting (HV-HPDC)

key_findings: The (Al-Zn-Mg)-Fe series alloys (HE700) stem from the Al - 4.75 w% Zn - 0.9 w% Mg - 1 w% Fe - 0.07 w% Ti castings which showed significant improvements over conventional Al-Si-Mg alloys for automotive structural applications.
notes: Composition extracted from Section 1. The alloy is described as a dilute hypoeutectic Al-Fe alloy system with addition of Zn and Mg. Processing method is HV-HPDC.

key_findings: Addition of 5.5 wt% Al increased the strength of Zn-Al alloy, reaching a maximum tensile strength of 308±8 MPa and yield strength of 240±7 MPa.
notes: Data extracted from Section 4. The values are reported as mean ± standard deviation (308±8 MPa for UTS, 240±7 MPa for YS). Composition is based on the addition of 5.5 wt% Al to Zn, balance Zn assumed.

paper_title: Effect of grain size on superplastic deformation behavior of Zn-0.033 Mg alloy
institution: School of Mechanical Engineering and Automation, Beihang University
key_findings: Zn-0.033Mg alloy with different grain sizes from 1.53 to 36.17 μm was obtained by equal-channel angular pressing and heat treatment.
The effect of grain size on superplasticity in Zn alloys and underlying mechanisms were investigated.
notes: Based on Section 3 of the provided text. Only composition (Mg 0.033 wt%) and grain size range (1.53-36.17 μm) are explicitly extractable. Processing method (ECAP + heat treatment) is mentioned but no numerical parameters given. Mechanical properties and testing conditions are not provided in this section.

key_findings: An Al-7.60Zn-2.55 Mg (wt.%) alloy with relatively low Mg content was reported to be hardened by T-phase.
notes: Data extracted from Section 1. Only composition and key finding about T-phase hardening are available; all other fields are null due to lack of evidence.

key_findings: Cold-rolled Zn-4Cu-0.02Li exhibited the best combination of corrosion resistance and mechanical properties among the prepared alloys.
Corrosion rate after immersion in SBF for 21 days was about 55 μm/year.
Yield strength of 256 MPa, ultimate strength of 342 MPa, fracture elongation of 39.8%.
Rolling improved the shape of CuZn4 second phase, resulting in a uniform corrosion mode.
notes: Data extracted from Sections 3, 6, and 11. Composition calculated as balance Zn (100 - 4 - 0.02 = 95.98 wt%). Corrosion rate unit assumed to be μm/year based on context (lm/year likely a typo for μm/year).

key_findings: Zn-1Cu-0.5Ag shows UTS of 332.90 MPa, YS of 318.70 MPa, and elongation of 26.32%.
Addition of 1wt% Ag leads to a slight decrease in hardness and strength but improves ductility.
notes: Data extracted from Section 4 of the provided text. Alloy is as-extruded.

corrosion_properties.corrosion_test_method: electrochemical test and immersion test in simulated body fluid (SBF) solution

key_findings: Ununiform heterostructure facilitates high strength without sacrificing ductility.
Strength improvement mainly from grain refinement strengthening and Orowan looping strengthening.
Degradation rate meets requirement for biodegradable implants.
notes: Based on Section 2 of the provided text. Specific composition values (wt%) for Cu and Ti are not given in the extracted text.

paper_title: Microstructure, mechanical properties, and damping capacity of high-Zn-content Al–Zn–Mg alloys with Sc and Zr additions
institution: Key Laboratory of New Processing Technology for Nonferrous Metal & Materials, Ministry of Education, Guilin University of Technology
key_findings: Primary and precipitated Al3(Sc,Zr) and Al3Zr phases could promote grain refinement more effectively than Al3Sc phase.
High Zn and low Mg contents lead to high thermal stability of η' phases during aging at high temperature and long time.
notes: Composition is nominal (Al–15Zn–0.5 Mg–Sc–Zr). Exact Sc and Zr contents not specified in extracted text. Paper published in Vacuum 216 (2023) 112491.

key_findings: As-cast Al-Zn-Mg alloy has a tensile strength of 421.4 MPa.
Melting point is 623.2 °C for as-cast Al-Zn-Mg alloys.
notes: Data extracted from Section 2 of the provided text. Composition details not specified.

key_findings: The work studied the effects of heat treatment on microstructural evolution and corrosion resistance of Al-Zn-Mg alloys containing Sc and Zr, focusing on Al3(Sc,Zr) precipitates morphology and distribution, texture, and recrystallization.
notes: Based on limited evidence from Section 1. Alloy A is the base Al-Zn-Mg alloy without Sc/Zr. Alloy B contains 0.19% Sc and 0.09% Zr. Exact Zn, Mg, Al compositions are not provided in the extracted text.

paper_title: Stability of damping capacity and mechanical properties of high-Zn-concentration Al–Zn–Mg–Sc alloy
institution: Key Laboratory of New Processing Technology for Nonferrous Metal & Materials, Ministry of Education, Guilin University of Technology, Guilin, 541004, China
key_findings: The Al–20Zn–0.5 Mg–0.5Sc alloy contained fine grains, a high number of solution atoms, Zn phases, Al3Sc phases, and high-density fine η and η' phases.
Decreasing the Zn content led to the disappearance of the Zn phases and the decrease of intragranular η phases density and solution atom number.
Increasing the Mg content did not increase the number of solution atoms.
notes: Data extracted from abstract and Section 14. Composition is nominal. Mechanical properties not provided in the extracted sections for this specific alloy.

key_findings: Low Zn/Mg ratio alloy exhibits T' and T phases
Peak hardness of Low-Zn/Mg alloy is 190.3 ± 0.8 HV
notes: Data extracted from Section 2. Composition details not provided in the given text. Hardness value is peak hardness.

key_findings: With increasing hot rolling reduction, the refinement degree of recrystallized structure increases, dissolution and fragmentation of second phase particles occur, and ultrafine sub-grains in shear bands form.
The number of spherical strain-induced precipitates and ultrafine sub-grains increases due to high dislocation density before T6 heat treatment.
After T6 heat treatment, the morphology of nanoprecipitates changes from rod-like to globular, and the mean size of nanoprecipitates decreases.
notes: Data extracted from Section 2 of the provided content, which describes the effects of cold rolling with different hot rolling reductions on Al–Zn–Mg–Cu (AA7050) alloy. Specific numerical values for composition, processing parameters, and mechanical properties were not provided in this section.

paper_title: Unraveling the leverage effect in phases stability and mechanical properties of Al-Zn-Mg-Cu alloy
institution: National Key Laboratory of Science and Technology for High–strength Structural Materials, Central South University, Changsha, China
key_findings: The study investigates the leverage effect of alloying element pairs (Al:Cu, Mg:Zn, Cu:Mg, Cu:Zn) on phase stability and mechanical properties of Al-Zn-Mg-Cu alloys.
Tuning relative ratios between Zn, Mg, Cu while maintaining total alloying amount is a widely adopted strategy to improve performance.
notes: Based on evidence from Section 3 only. The paper is an abstract/introduction; no specific numerical data on composition, processing, or properties are provided in the extracted text.

key_findings: The Al-Zn-Mg-Sc alloy after heat treatment H6 achieved UTS of 390.3 MPa, YS of 312.9 MPa, and elongation of 25.9%.
notes: Data extracted from Section 11, Table 4. The specific composition (wt%) of Zn, Mg, Al, and Sc is not provided in the extracted text. The processing condition 'H6' is a heat treatment state but its parameters are not detailed in the provided sections.

paper_title: Optimization of mechanical properties of dilute Mg-Zn-Y alloys prepared by rapid solidification
institution: Magnesium Research Center, Kumamoto University; Department of Physics of Materials, Charles University; Nuclear Physics Institute of the Czech Academy of Science
key_findings: RS ribbon-consolidated Mg-Zn-Y alloys have shown exceptional mechanical properties.
Dilution and variation in metal flow rate are used for tailoring microstructure and mechanical properties.
RS ribbon-consolidation results in fine grain (700–1200 nm) microstructure.
Bimodal microstructure with a specific distribution of internal strain leads to improved mechanical properties.
notes: Data extracted from Section 17 (table) and Section 2 (abstract/highlights). Composition is in at.%. Grain size and mechanical properties correspond to the first row of the table (Vr=2.5, mfr=0.7).

paper_title: Microstructure and mechanical properties of as-cast Mg-Sn-Zn-Y alloys
institution: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Chongqing University; National Engineering Research Center for Magnesium Alloys, Chongqing University
key_findings: Y addition refines grains and promotes precipitation of MgSnY phase with high stability.
Increasing Y from 0.2 to 0.8 wt% increases amount and size of MgSnY phase.
MgZn2 phase tends to precipitate attached to Mg2Sn phase due to mismatch.
notes: Yttrium content varied between 0.2 and 0.8 wt% but exact values per alloy not specified in provided text. Mechanical properties not numerically reported in the extracted sections.

key_findings: Grain refinement strengthening and precipitation strengthening are the main strengthening mechanisms.
Al3(Sc,Zr) particles act as heterogeneous nucleation sites for Al grains, resulting in grain refinement.
Nanoscale Al3(Sc,Zr) particles can precipitate homogeneously after proper heat treatment and effectively pin grain boundaries, enhancing strength.
notes: Based on Section 1 only. The paper discusses high-pressure torsion (HPT) and natural aging of an Al-Zn-Mg-Cu-Zr-Sc alloy, but no specific numerical values for composition or mechanical properties were provided in the extracted text.

key_findings: Tensile strength decreases in order: T4 (558.8 MPa) > T6 (544.7 MPa) > T6I4 (536.8 MPa).
Elongation increases in order: T4 (1.7%) < T6 (2.2%) < T6I4 (2.5%).
T4 state: highest tensile strength due to GP zone cutting strengthening and GB blocking; lowest ductility due to MgSi-saturated phases and wide PFZ.
T6 state: GP zones transform to uniform spherical η phases; GB contain discontinuous MgSi-saturated phases with no obvious PFZ; balance of strength and toughness.
T6I4 state: GP zones grow at high temperature then mature at low temperature, forming elongated η phases; clean GB without noticeable precipitates.
notes: Data extracted from Section 1 of the provided paper content. Only one alloy system with varying aging treatments (T4, T6, T6I4) is described. Composition details are not provided in the extracted text.

key_findings: Filler metal with high Mg and low Mn proportion can enhance the strength of Al-Zn-Mg alloys.
Tensile specimens of weldments made with ER5356 broke in the fusion zone adjacent to the fusion boundary, except for T73-treated specimens.
T73 heat treatment enhanced fusion zone strength, causing cracks to occur in HAZ and base metal only.
AA7003 and AA7005 after T6 heat treatment showed the highest ultimate tensile strength (UTS).
A linear relationship exists between UTS and hardness for AA7005 after artificial aging at 120°C for hardness above 50 HV.
notes: Data extracted from Section 1 of the provided paper content. Specific numerical values for mechanical properties are not provided in the text; only qualitative comparisons are given.

key_findings: Zn-5WE43 showed the highest tensile strength of 335.4 MPa, but the elongation was only 1%
The compressive strength of Zn-5WE43 porous scaffolds was 73.2 MPa
The formation of brittle MgZn2 deteriorated strength in Zn-8WE43
notes: Data extracted from Section 19. The alloy is a porous scaffold for biodegradable application. Composition details (e.g., exact wt% of WE43 elements) are not provided in the extracted text.

key_findings: Al-Zn-Mg alloy with 4.19% Zn, 1.36% Mg, 0.32% Mn, 0.21% Cr, 0.08% Zr, 0.05% Ti, 0.08% Fe, 0.02% Si (wt%) was used.
Homogenization at 470°C for 24 h, preheated at 450°C for 1 h, extruded with ratio 22:1, water quenched, then pre-aged at 100°C for 12 h.
Stress-aging at 160°C with ±125 MPa was performed.
notes: Data extracted from Section 6 of the provided content. Composition and processing details are from an in-house made Al-Zn-Mg alloy. No mechanical or corrosion test results were provided in this section.

paper_title: Effects of Sn addition on microstructure and mechanical properties of Mg-Zn-Al alloys
institution: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Chongqing University; National Engineering Research Center for Magnesium Alloys, Chongqing University
key_findings: Sn addition (0, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 3 wt%) to Mg-4Zn-1.5Al alloy was investigated.
As-cast Mg-4Zn-1.5Al alloy consists of α-Mg and Mg32(Al,Zn)49 phases.
Sn-containing alloys consist of α-Mg, Mg32(Al,Zn)49, and Mg2Sn phases.
Mg32(Al,Zn)49 phase was not detected after extrusion due to dissolution during homogenization.
notes: Based on limited evidence from Section 2. Sn content varied but exact values for each variant not specified in extracted text. Mechanical properties of as-extruded alloys were studied but numerical values not provided in the extracted abstract.

key_findings: Zn/Mg ratio of 11.4 results in dense and continuously distributed grain boundary precipitates.
Tensile strength is 333.2 MPa, yield strength is 269.4 MPa, elongation is 18.4%.
notes: Data extracted from Section 1 of the provided text. Zn/Mg ratio is 11.4. Alloy designation 1#.

paper_title: Effects of Sn addition on microstructure and mechanical properties of Mg-Zn-Al alloys
institution: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Chongqing University; National Engineering Research Center for Magnesium Alloys, Chongqing University
key_findings: As-cast Mg-4Zn-1.5Al alloy composed of α-Mg and Mg32(Al,Zn)49 phases.
Sn-containing alloys consisted of α-Mg, Mg32(Al,Zn)49 and Mg2Sn phases.
Mg32(Al,Zn)49 was not detected after extruding due to dissolution during homogenization.
notes: Sn addition levels studied: 0, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0 wt%. Mechanical properties of as-extruded alloys were studied but numerical values not provided in extracted text.

key_findings: The mass Zn/Mg ratio of Mg(Zn,Al)2 precipitates is nearly 5.4:1.
For Zn+Mg ≈ 7 wt%, the density of Mg(Zn,Al)2 precipitates after two-stage aging was highest with a Zn/Mg ratio of 4.53.
High Zn/Mg ratios (6.32 and 12.65) resulted in reduced precipitate density due to low Mg content.
High Zn/Mg ratio alloys exhibited wider precipitate-free zones (PFZs) after two-stage over-aging.
Low Zn/Mg ratio alloys achieved higher strength due to a higher density of fine precipitates.
notes: Data extracted from Section 1 of the provided text. The alloy is an Al-Zn-Mg system with a specific focus on the Zn/Mg ratio of 4.53. No specific alloy designation, year, or other metadata was provided in the evidence.

paper_title: Influence of Zn/Mg ratio on mechanical strength and corrosion resistance of Al-Zn-Mg alloys
institution: High-Performance Metal Structural Materials Research Institute, Shagang School of Iron and Steel, Soochow University
key_findings: Alloys with Zn/Mg atomic ratios of 0.53–2.51 were systematically analyzed.
As the Zn/Mg ratio increases, mechanical strength initially improves and peaked at a ratio of 1.30, with a yield strength of 536.93 MPa.
Corrosion resistance decreases with a Zn/Mg ratio of 1.94.
A critical Zn/Mg atomic ratio of 1.30 is identified for the formation of T' or η' during artificial aging.
The Zn/Mg ratio governs both strength and corrosion resistance by affecting precipitation characteristics and grain-boundary microstructures.
notes: Data extracted primarily from Section 13 and Section 4. The paper title and year are inferred from the journal context (Journal of Alloys and Compounds, 2025). Aging temperature and time are from Section 17 (120°C/24h artificial aging). Yield strength is from the abstract of Section 13. Corrosion test method is from Section 4.

key_findings: After solution and aging treatment (H6), the ultimate tensile strength of the Al-Zn-Mg-Sc alloy increased from 269.4 MPa to 392.9 MPa, and elongation increased from 12.7% to 30.2%.
notes: Data extracted from Section 11 and Section 13. The alloy is laser melted Al-Zn-Mg-Sc. The specific composition (e.g., Al-6.5Zn-2.2Mg-0.4Sc) is mentioned in Section 11 but not directly linked to the H6 treatment mechanical properties table in Section 13. The UTS and YS values are from Table 4 in Section 13 for the H6 sample.

key_findings: As-extruded Zn-3Cu-0.5Fe alloy showed favorable UTS, YTS, and elongation of 269, 302 MPa, and 25% by optimizing extrusion temperature.
notes: Data extracted from Section 8. UTS and YTS values appear swapped in the original text (UTS=269, YTS=302), but extracted as written.

key_findings: As-cast Zn–1.6 Mg consisted of α–Zn phase and a three-component eutectic structure (α–Zn + Mg2Zn11 + MgZn2).
MgZn2 particles with nanoscale dimensions were precipitated within Mg2Zn11 phase.
Homogenization at 340°C for 10 h transforms MgZn2 into equilibrium eutectic phase Mg2Zn11.
notes: Data extracted from Section 12. Composition is based on 'Zn–1.6 Mg' notation, interpreted as 1.6 wt% Mg, balance Zn. Homogenization parameters are provided but not listed under processing as they are not explicitly linked to this specific alloy in a structured table.

key_findings: Zn-1Cu-0.5Ag shows UTS of 332.90 MPa, YS of 318.70 MPa, and elongation of 26.32%.
Addition of 1wt% Ag leads to a slight decrease in hardness and strength but improves ductility.
Ductile fracture morphology with deep dimples observed.
notes: Data extracted from Section 7. Alloy is as-extruded. Hardness is Vickers (HV).

key_findings: Al-9.2Zn-2.2Mg-2.3Cu-0.2Sc-0.15Zr (7E36) alloy has highest peak strength with UTS 773 MPa, YS 758 MPa, elongation 11.5%
Increasing Zn content leads to weaker solid solution
notes: Data extracted from Section 7. Paper accepted Feb 2023.

key_findings: As-extruded Zn-Mg binary alloy with 0.15 wt% Mg has UTS of 250 MPa and elongation of 22%.
notes: Data extracted from Section 1. The alloy is as-extruded. Mg content is 0.15 wt%.

paper_title: Effect of heat treatments on the microstructure and mechanical properties of Zn-15 wt% Al alloy
institution: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering, and Materials Science, Zaozhuang University; Shaoxing Tianlong Tin Materials Co., Ltd
key_findings: Annealing below 275 °C results in granular α particles homogeneously distributed in η phase matrix.
Annealing above 275 °C followed by air cooling produces a lamellar microstructure with isolated η grains.
notes: Data extracted from Section 12. Only composition and annealing temperature are explicitly provided; mechanical properties are not given in the extracted text.

key_findings: Filler metal with high Mg and low Mn proportion can enhance strength of Al-Zn-Mg alloys.
T6 heat treatment gave highest UTS for AA7003 and AA7005.
T73 treatment enhanced fusion zone strength, causing cracks in HAZ and base metal only.
Linear relationship exists between UTS and hardness for AA7005 after AA at 120°C (hardness above 50 HV).
notes: Data extracted from Section 1 only. Specific numerical values for mechanical properties not provided in this section. Aging temperature of 120°C inferred from AA7005 data.

key_findings: The addition of Zn in Mg could improve its mechanical performance and biocompatibility.
The tensile strength and elongation of Mg-6Zn alloy could reach 279.5 MPa and 18.8 %, respectively.
notes: Data extracted from Section 3. Composition is inferred as balance Mg with 6 wt% Zn. Paper content mentions 'Mg-6Zn alloy'.

key_findings: As-cast Al-Zn-Mg-0.89%Cu alloy has UTS 448 ± 6 MPa, YS 253 ± 5 MPa, uniform elongation 3.5 ± 0.3%, total elongation 3.5 ± 0.4%.
notes: Data extracted from Section 15, Table 6. Composition is based on Cu content only; other elements (Zn, Mg, Al) not specified in the extracted text. Year inferred from journal volume (2024).

key_findings: The ultimate tensile strength of as-extruded Zn-Mg binary alloys increased from 250 to 340 MPa, but the elongation dramatically decreased from 22% to 6% with increasing Mg content from 0.15 to 1 wt%, which is attributed to the collective role of grain refinement and second phase (Mg2Zn11).
notes: Based on limited evidence from Section 1. The paper content discusses Zn-Mg binary alloys but does not provide a specific alloy designation, full composition (Zn balance), or detailed processing parameters. The Mg content range is 0.15-1 wt%. Mechanical properties are given as ranges, not for a single alloy. Only the second phase Mg2Zn11 is mentioned.

key_findings: As-extruded Zn-2Cu-0.01Ti-0.38Li alloy possesses tensile yield strength of 408 MPa, ultimate tensile strength of 431 MPa, tensile elongation of 68%, compression yield strength of 393 MPa, and ultimate compression strength of 1030 MPa, showing the best comprehensive mechanical properties.
Adding Ti and Li to Zn-Cu alloy can reduce and improve its corrosion resistance, respectively.
notes: Data extracted from Section 19. The alloy is as-extruded. Corrosion rate values not provided in the extracted text.

paper_title: Evolution of grain size and texture of Zn-0.5Cu ECAP alloy during annealing at 200 ℃ and its impact on mechanical properties
institution: Hohai University
key_findings: The paper studies the evolution of grain size and texture of Zn-0.5Cu ECAP alloy during annealing at 200°C and its impact on mechanical properties.
notes: Data extracted from Section 10. Only the alloy system, composition (Cu 0.5 wt%), annealing temperature (200°C), and paper metadata are available. Mechanical properties and other processing details are not provided in the given evidence.

key_findings: Mg addition decreased Zn peak intensity and led to appearance of h peaks in XRD
Elongated grains observed after hot rolling; grain diameter slightly decreased with increasing Mg content
notes: Data extracted from Section 1. Composition xMg is variable and not specified numerically. T6 treatment: 120°C for 17h. Strain rate for tensile test: 5e-2 s^-1.

key_findings: Mg was the most strengthening additive among Cu, Ti, Mn, Ca, or Mg for Zn.
A 0.3 wt.% Mg addition improved the 0.2% yield strength of Zn by more than 5 times, from 36 to 200 MPa.
Mg caused a significant reduction in elongation and bendability.
notes: Data extracted from Section 2. Composition is inferred as balance Zn with 0.3 wt% Mg. Yield strength is 0.2% proof stress.

key_findings: Hot rolling refines grain size due to severe plastic deformation and dynamic recrystallization.
High alloy content (1.50Cu, 1.06Ti) leads to larger second phases and increased brittleness.
Hot-rolled Zn-Cu-Ti alloys have superior corrosion resistance compared to as-cast alloys.
notes: Data extracted from Section 1 of the provided text. Only composition and qualitative findings are available; quantitative mechanical and corrosion values are missing.

paper_title: Effects of Ti on microstructure, mechanical properties and biodegradation behavior of Zn-Cu alloy
institution: Key Laboratory for Microstructure Control of Metallic Materials of Jiangxi Province, Nanchang Hangkong University
key_findings: Zn-2Cu alloy consisted of Zn matrix and CuZn5 phase; TiZn16 phase newly formed with Ti addition.
As Ti content increased, CuZn5 phase was further refined; TiZn16 phase gradually changed from fibrous to discontinuous reticular morphology.

Zn-2Cu-0.05Ti alloy exhibited the optimal mechanical properties: yield strength, compressive yield strength, ultimate tensile strength and elongation increased by 37.2%, 52.9%, 38.7% and 19.1% respectively, compared with Zn-2Cu alloy.

Specific numerical values for the mechanical properties of the base Zn-2Cu alloy are not provided in the extracted text, only the percentage improvements. The alloy_id is constructed based on the composition Zn-2Cu-0.05Ti.

key_findings: The as-extruded Zn-2Cu-0.01Ti-0.38Li alloy possesses tensile yield strength of 408 MPa, ultimate tensile strength of 431 MPa, tensile elongation of 68%, compression yield strength of 393 MPa, and ultimate compression strength of 1030 MPa, showing the best comprehensive mechanical properties.
Adding Ti and Li to Zn-Cu alloy can reduce and improve its corrosion resistance, respectively.

processing.casting_method: poured into steel mold preheated at 150 °C

corrosion_properties.corrosion_test_method: immersion tests and electrochemical measurements in Hank's solution (pH 7.4) at 37 ± 0.5 °C

key_findings: The effects of Ti on microstructure, mechanical properties and biodegradation behavior of Zn-2Cu alloy were investigated.
notes: Data extracted from Section 1. Only the base alloy Zn-2Cu-0Ti is represented here. Ti content varies (0, 0.05, 0.1 wt%) in the study. No numerical mechanical or corrosion results are provided in the given text.

key_findings: Addition of 0.05 wt% Ti to Zn-2Cu alloy increased compressive yield strength by 52.9% compared to Zn-2Cu alloy.
The improvement is attributed to refinement of CuZn5 phase and formation of fibrous TiZn16 phase.
Further addition of Ti (0.1 wt%) led to a reduction in both strength and ductility.
notes: Data extracted from Section 2. Compressive yield strength (CYT) was measured, but numerical values for CYT are not provided in the text. Only the percentage increase is given.

key_findings: The as-extruded Zn-2Cu-0.01Ti-0.38Li alloy possesses tensile yield strength of 408 ± 16.6 MPa, ultimate tensile strength of 431 ± 7.9 MPa, and tensile elongation of 68 ± 4.05%.
Adding Ti and Li to Zn-Cu alloy can reduce and improve its corrosion resistance, respectively.
notes: Data extracted from Section 17. The alloy is described as having the best comprehensive mechanical properties among those studied. Processing condition is 'as-extruded'.

key_findings: Addition of 0.3 wt.% Mg improved the 0.2% yield strength of Zn by more than 5 times, from 36 to 200 MPa.
Mg addition caused a significant reduction in elongation and bendability.
notes: Data extracted from Section 2. The yield strength is 0.2% proof yield strength. Composition is based on the stated addition of 0.3 wt.% Mg to pure Zn (balance Zn).

key_findings: Superplastic elongation of 376 ± 79% achieved at 453 K and 0.001 s⁻¹.
Grain boundary sliding is the dominant superplastic deformation mechanism.
Deformation accommodated by {1012} tensile twins (null86 ± 5°〈1120〉twins).
Flow stress significantly reduced with increasing deformation temperature.
Elongation effectively improved with reduction in strain rate.
notes: Data extracted from Sections 1 and 19. Elongation value is the mean (376%) from the range 376 ± 79%. Grain size of 1.53 μm from Section 19. Alloy composition Zn-0.033Mg (wt%) from Section 17.

key_findings: The effect of Cu addition (0.25, 0.5, 0.75 wt.%) on the microstructure, mechanical properties and degradation rate of Mg-2Gd were investigated.

key_findings: Compared to Mg-5Nd alloy, YS, UTS and elongation of Mg-5Nd-3.0Al alloy are enhanced by about 45.3%, 72.4% and 264.0%, respectively.

key_findings: Large-scale Mg-8Gd-4Y-1Zn-Mn (wt.%) alloy ingot with diameter 315 mm and length 2410 mm prepared via semi-continuous casting.
No obvious macro segregation detected.
Main eutectic structures: α-Mg, Mg3RE-type, Mg5RE-type, and LPSO phases.
Unusual casting twins (10-12 extension and 10-11 compression) observed at ingot edge due to intensive internal stress.
T4 heat treatment eliminated micro segregation; remaining phases were α-Mg and LPSO.
T6 samples exhibited improved mechanical properties at ambient temperature due to dense prismatic β' precipitates and profuse basal precipitates.

key_findings: Yield strength increases from 307 MPa to 342 MPa as Zn/Mg ratio rises from 3.68 to 6.11.
Precipitation strengthening is the dominant factor influencing yield strength.
Higher Zn/Mg ratio results in narrower PFZ, which contributes positively to corrosion resistance.
Highest Zn/Mg ratio of 6.11 exhibits the greatest corrosion depth, indicating GBPs dominate intergranular corrosion resistance.
Higher Zn/Mg ratios exhibit finer grain boundary precipitates, leading to poorer stress corrosion resistance.
notes: Data extracted from Section 1 of the provided paper content. Specific composition values (Zn, Mg, Al wt%) are not provided; only Zn/Mg ratios are given. Mechanical properties (yield strength range) are reported for varying Zn/Mg ratios.

paper_title: Effects of Er/Zr Micro-Alloying and Zn/Mg Ratio on Wear Resistance of Al-Zn-Mg Alloys
institution: Weiqiao Lightweight Research Center at Soochow, Suzhou 215001, China
key_findings: Reducing the Zn/Mg ratio mitigated wear volume across all tested loads.
For the Al4.5Zn1.5Mg alloy, Er microalloying significantly reduced wear volume under moderate-to-low loads (30 N, 50 N).
At 30 N load, wear mechanisms: abrasive, fatigue, and adhesive wear.
At 50 N load, abrasive wear dominated, accompanied by fatigue and adhesive wear.
At 70 N load, primary mechanisms: abrasive and fatigue wear, with additional adhesive and oxidative wear.
notes: Based on limited evidence from Sections 1, 3, and 14. The alloy Al4.5Zn1.5Mg corresponds to a Zn/Mg ratio of 3:1 with total Zn+Mg = 6 wt%. Er content is 0.1 wt% as per Section 1. Zr presence is mentioned but exact content not specified. Year inferred from paper metadata (Materials 2025, 18, 3541).

key_findings: Applied tensile stress promotes formation of plate-shaped GP II zones on {111} plane in early aging of 7475 alloy.

Tensile stress decelerates coarsening of precipitates, delaying peak-aging and over-aging.
Compressive stress accelerates atom diffusion but over-aging is slightly slowed due to competitive growth among high-density precipitates.

paper_title: Effects of Ti on microstructure, mechanical properties and biodegradation behavior of Zn-Cu alloy
institution: Key Laboratory for Microstructure Control of Metallic Materials of Jiangxi Province, Nanchang Hangkong University
key_findings: Zn-2Cu alloy consisted of Zn matrix and CuZn5 phase; TiZn16 phase newly formed with Ti addition.
As Ti content increased, CuZn5 phase was further refined; TiZn16 phase gradually changed from fibrous to discontinuous reticular morphology.
Zn-2Cu-0.05Ti alloy exhibited the optimal mechanical properties.
Yield strength, compressive yield strength, ultimate tensile strength and elongation increased by 37.2%, 52.9%, 38.7% and 19.1% respectively, compared with Zn-2Cu alloy.
With Ti addition, the degradation rates of the alloys were greatly improved towards the clinical requirements.
notes: Based on evidence from Sections 2 and 8. The paper title and authors are from Section 8. The specific mechanical property values (yield strength, UTS, elongation) for the Zn-2Cu-0.05Ti alloy are not provided as absolute numbers in the extracted text, only the percentage increases relative to Zn-2Cu. paper_title: Mg-Zn-Y-Nd alloy for degradable orthopedic implants
key_findings: Zn content change exerts little influence on grain structure of extruded Mg-Zn-Y-Nd alloy.
Increase of Y content hinders dynamic recrystallization and growth of DRXed grains, decreasing size and volume fraction of equiaxed DRXed grains.
Tensile and compressive properties are little affected by Zn content due to similar grain structure.
As Y content increases, tensile yield strength (TYS) and ultimate strength (TUS) increase while elongation decreases.
notes: Based on Section 1 of the provided evidence. Specific numerical values for composition and mechanical properties are not provided in the extracted text.

paper_title: Effect of different Zn concentrations on the microstructure, corrosion property, and mechanical property of Mg-0.3Sc-xZn alloys
key_findings: Mg-0.3Sc-1Zn alloy exhibits the highest corrosion resistance (0.194 mm/y) among the studied compositions.
The alloy has appropriate mechanical properties with an ultimate tensile strength of 228 MPa and elongation of 19%.

With increasing Zn content, the ScZn phase forms and intensifies galvanic corrosion, reducing corrosion performance.

key_findings: Addition of Mg forms Mg2Zn11 phase, volume fraction increases with Mg content.

key_findings: Large-scale Mg-8Gd-4Y-1Zn-Mn (wt.%) alloy ingot with diameter 315 mm and length 2410 mm prepared via semi-continuous casting.

T6 samples showed improved mechanical properties at ambient temperature due to dense prismatic β' precipitates and profuse basal precipitates.

key_findings: Zn/Mg ratio strongly governs aging response and age-hardening ability.

At higher Zn/Mg ratios, age-hardening ability decreases, indicating an optimal Zn/Mg ratio for strengthening.
notes: Data extracted from Section 8. Alloy ZM43 (Zn/Mg = 1.3) is identified as having optimal peak hardness. Specific composition values (wt%) are not provided in the text.

key_findings: The as-extruded Mg-Zn-Y-Nd alloys are mainly composed of fine dynamic recrystallized grains (DRXed grains), large unDRXed grains and linearly distributed secondary phases.

key_findings: With increasing Zn/Mg ratios, the ageing process is significantly accelerated and the time to peak ageing is reduced.
T' phase predominates in alloys of lower Zn/Mg ratios while η' phase predominates in alloys with a Zn/Mg ratio over 2.86.
Co-existence of T' phase and η' phase with a large number density is beneficial to the high strength of alloys.
A strength model has been established to predict the co-strengthening effect of T' phase and η' phase in Al-Zn-Mg-Cu alloys.

key_findings: Filler metal with high Mg and low Mn proportion can enhance strength of Al-Zn-Mg alloys.

T6 heat treatment results in highest UTS for AA7003 and AA7005.

key_findings: Mg content affects precipitation behavior and mechanical properties of high Zn-content Al-Zn alloy.
Dynamic precipitation of Zn and η phases occurs during hot rolling of Al-Zn-Mg alloys.
Zn/Mg ratio significantly influences aging process, precipitate types (T' vs η'), and mechanical strength.
Co-existence of T' and η' phases with high number density is beneficial for high strength.
Cu addition improves strength primarily through precipitation strengthening.
Variable-rate non-isothermal aging can achieve combination of high strength and corrosion resistance.
Sc/Er co-addition improves yield strength, elongation, and corrosion performance.
Trace Ce addition improves corrosion resistance with little effect on strength.
Optimal Zn/Mg ratio (e.g., 1.30) yields peak mechanical strength; corrosion resistance decreases at higher ratios.
Decreasing Zn/Mg ratio improves strength and corrosion resistance by refining precipitates and grain boundary distribution.
Te addition refines grains and improves corrosion resistance and mechanical properties.
Lower Zn/Mg ratio increases volume fraction of matrix precipitates, enhancing strength.
Narrower PFZ and lower Zn concentration in matrix improve SCC and electrochemical corrosion resistance.
notes: The provided text consists of multiple paper abstracts and excerpts, but no single paper's full data (composition, processing parameters, exact mechanical values) is completely extractable. The data is aggregated from multiple sources on Al-Zn-Mg and Al-Zn-Mg-Cu alloys. Numerical values for specific alloys (e.g., Al-9.2Zn-2.2Mg-2.3Cu-0.2Sc-0.15Zr with UTS 773 MPa, YS 758 MPa, elongation 11.5%) are mentioned but cannot be assigned to a single coherent paper entry without a specific paper title or DOI. Therefore, most fields are set to null.

corrosion_properties.corrosion_test_method: EXCO (exfoliation corrosion) test, intergranular corrosion depth measurement

key_findings: Alloys with Zn/Mg ratio of 6.11 had a tensile strength of 394 MPa, 38 MPa higher than alloys with Zn/Mg ratio of 3.68.
Continuous grain boundary precipitate distribution in the highest Zn/Mg ratio alloy resulted in a peak intergranular corrosion depth of 52.5 µm and stress corrosion sensitivity index of 6.1%.
Alloy with Zn/Mg ratio of 3.68 had intergranular corrosion depth of 15.3 µm and stress corrosion sensitivity index of 3.6% due to coarse and discontinuous grain boundary precipitates.
Tensile properties are highly dependent on intragranular precipitation, while corrosion behavior corresponds to grain boundary precipitation characteristics.

key_findings: Increasing Mg content (at constant Zn 8.5 wt.%) leads to higher hardness and strength but lower conductivity due to higher volume fraction of matrix precipitates.
Increasing Mg content enlarges area fraction of grain boundary precipitates (GBPs), promoting intergranular fracture and decreasing toughness.

Decreasing Zn/Mg ratio enhances stress corrosion cracking (SCC) resistance due to narrower precipitate-free zones (PFZ) and lower Zn content in matrix.
Cu content contribution to yield strength is predominantly via precipitation strengthening, not grain boundary or solid solution strengthening.
