key_findings: Mg segregation at grain boundary becomes severer with the increase of non-isothermal heat treatment times, and the tensile strength and elongation of the alloy decrease gradually.
The size and quantity of MgZn2 precipitation decrease after one time of non-isothermal heat treatment, but increase significantly after two and three times of non-isothermal heat treatment.
notes: The paper focuses on cyclic non-isothermal heat treatment of Al-Zn-Mg alloy. Composition details are referenced to be in table 1 of the original paper but not provided in the extracted text. Solution treatment at 470°C for 1 hour followed by water quenching is mentioned for the T4 state prior to NIHT.

paper_title: Significant improvement in mechanical properties of Mg-Zn-La alloy by minor Ca addition
institution: School of Materials Science and Technology, Xi'an University of Technology
key_findings: Small addition of Ca in Mg-Zn-La alloy induced the formation of Ca2Mg6Zn3 phase at grain boundaries in the as-cast state.
Ca addition promoted dynamic precipitation in the as-extruded state.
Fine precipitates postponed dynamic recrystallization and refined DRXed grains for Ca containing alloy.
Small addition of Ca did not affect the texture of the alloy.
notes: Based on Section 8 of the provided text. Exact compositions (wt%) of Zn, La, and Ca are not specified in the extracted text. The paper is from Materials Characterization, 2020.

paper_title: Effect of Zn/Mg ratio on precipitation and strengthening of Al–Zn–Mg alloys after aging
institution: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Central South University, Changsha, China
key_findings: The influence of Zn/Mg ratio (1.2-3.5) on the precipitation and strengthening effect of Al–Zn–Mg (Zn + Mg = 10 wt%) alloys aged at 130°C was investigated.
The hardness of the peak-aged alloys tends to increase with the increase of Zn/Mg ratio.
For as-quenched samples, with increase of Zn/Mg ratio, yield strength increases gradually, ultimate strength increases first then decreases slightly, elongation decreases gradually.
After peak-aging, with increase of Zn/Mg ratio, yield strength increases sharply then remains almost unchanged, tensile strength first increases sharply then slightly, elongation decreases gradually.
The increment of yield strength (ΔRp0.2) after aging increases when Zn/Mg ratio increases from 1.2 to 2.5, then decreases slightly with ratio increasing to 3.5.
notes: Data extracted from Section 3 and Section 9. Specific numerical values for mechanical properties are not provided in the extracted text, only trends. Composition is given as Zn+Mg = 10 wt% with Zn/Mg ratio varying from 1.2 to 3.5.

paper_title: Optimization of mechanical properties of dilute Mg-Zn-Y alloys prepared by rapid solidification
institution: Magnesium Research Center, Kumamoto University; Department of Physics of Materials, Charles University; Nuclear Physics Institute of the Czech Academy of Science; Graduated Student, Department of Materials Science, Kumamoto University
key_findings: RS ribbon-consolidated Mg-Zn-Y alloys have shown exceptional mechanical properties.
Dilution and variation in metal flow rate are used for tailoring microstructure and mechanical properties.
RS ribbon-consolidation results in fine grain (700–1200 nm) microstructure.
Bimodal microstructure with a specific distribution of internal strain leads to improved mechanical properties.
notes: Grain size is estimated as the midpoint of the reported range 700–1200 nm. Composition details (Zn, Y wt%) are not provided in the extracted sections.

paper_title: Microstructure and mechanical properties of as-cast Mg-Sn-Zn-Y alloys
institution: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Chongqing University, Chongqing 400044, China
key_findings: Addition of Y to Mg-5Sn-3Zn refines grains and promotes precipitation of MgSnY phase with high stability.
Increasing Y addition from 0.2 to 0.8 wt% increases the amount and size of MgSnY phase.
MgZn2 phase tends to precipitate attached to Mg2Sn phase due to mismatch.
notes: Composition is based on Mg-5Sn-3Zn base alloy with Y addition varied from 0.2 to 0.8 wt%. Exact Y content not specified in the extracted text for a single alloy. Mechanical properties not provided in the extracted section.

paper_title: Effects of Sn addition on microstructure and mechanical properties of Mg-Zn-Al alloys
institution: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Chongqing University; National Engineering Research Center for Magnesium Alloys, Chongqing University

microstructure.texture: basal texture intensity reduced with increasing Sn content

key_findings: Sn addition refined grains of as-cast and as-extruded Mg-Zn-Al alloys
Basal texture intensity reduced with increasing Sn content
Sn addition improved tensile strength but had a harmful effect on ductility
At 2 wt% Sn, UTS=280 MPa, YS=147 MPa, elongation=17.4%
notes: Data extracted from Section 6 and Section 10. The alloy is as-extruded Mg-4Zn-1.5Al-2Sn. Mg32(Al, Zn)49 phase was not detected after extrusion due to dissolution during homogenization.

key_findings: Tensile strength under different aging treatments decreases in order: T4 (558.8 MPa) > T6 (544.7 MPa) > T6I4 (536.8 MPa).
Elongation shows reverse trend: T4 (1.7%) < T6 (2.2%) < T6I4 (2.5%).
High Zn/Mg ratio alloy exhibits faster aging response but lower peak hardness.
Peak hardness values: Low-Zn/Mg alloy 190.3 ± 0.8 HV, Mid-Zn/Mg alloy 188.6 ± 1.8 HV, High-Zn/Mg alloy 185.5 ± 1.1 HV.
Two-step aging (120°C for 6 h + 165°C for 12 h) achieved tensile strength 718 MPa and yield strength 654 MPa in Al-Zn-Mg-Cu alloy.
Multi-stage aging can achieve strength-toughness trade-off; GP zones and η' phases are key microstructural features.
Mg2Si is the main strengthening phase in Al-Zn-Mg-Si-Sc-Zr alloy, with as-built UTS of 383 MPa and elongation 9.5%.
Stress-aging alters precipitation kinetics, increasing number density of precipitates.
notes: Data extracted from multiple sections of a research paper compilation. Specific alloy compositions and processing parameters are not provided in the given text. Mechanical property values are from different alloys and treatments; no single alloy has complete property set.

paper_title: Microstructure and mechanical properties of Mg97Y2Zn1 alloy sheets processed by hot rolling
institution: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Central South University, Changsha, 410083, China
key_findings: Pre-rolled alloy microstructure consists of α-Mg matrix, block 18R LPSO phase at grain boundary, and lamellar 14H LPSO phase in grain interior.
After rolling at 450°C and 500°C, both 18R and 14H LPSO phases show kinks.
Alloy rolled at 500°C produces serious DRX, related to 14H LPSO phase.
notes: Based on Section 15. Composition is nominal (Mg97Y2Zn1). Rolling temperatures were 450°C and 500°C. Mechanical properties not numerically reported in the extracted text.

key_findings: Addition of 3.0 wt% Al to Mg-5Nd alloy reduced average grain size from 448 μm to 68 μm.
Grain refinement attributed to formation of Al2Nd particles acting as effective grain refiners.
Yield strength, UTS, and elongation enhanced by 45.3%, 72.4%, and 264.0% respectively compared to Mg-5Nd alloy.
Improvement in mechanical properties ascribed to refined grains and secondary phase strengthening.
notes: Data extracted from Section 1 and Section 15. Grain size of Mg-5Nd-3.0Al alloy is 68 μm (from Section 15). Mechanical properties (UTS 212 MPa, YS 93 MPa, elongation 9.1%) are for Mg-5Nd-3.0Al alloy (from Section 1).

key_findings: Zn/Mg ratio of 11.4 results in dense and continuously distributed grain boundary precipitates.
Tensile strength is 333.2 MPa, yield strength is 269.4 MPa, elongation is 18.4%.
notes: Alloy 1# from Materials 2020, 13, 3299. Zn/Mg ratio = 11.4. Data extracted from Section 1 and Section 14.

key_findings: The mass Zn/Mg ratio of Mg(Zn,Al)2 precipitates is nearly 5.4:1.
In Zn+Mg ≈ 7 wt% alloys, the density of Mg(Zn,Al)2 precipitates after two-stage aging is highest at a Zn/Mg ratio of 4.53.
High Zn/Mg ratios (6.32 and 12.65) reduce precipitate density due to low Mg content.
High Zn/Mg ratio alloys exhibit wider precipitation free zones (PFZ) after two-stage over-aging.
Low Zn/Mg ratio alloys achieve higher strength due to a higher density of fine precipitates.
notes: Data extracted from Section 1 of the provided text. The paper discusses Al-Zn-Mg alloys but does not provide specific alloy compositions or numerical mechanical properties in this section. The Zn/Mg ratios mentioned (4.53, 6.32, 12.65) are ratios, not absolute wt% values.

corrosion_properties.corrosion_test_method: weight loss per ASTM-G31-12a, immersion in 70°C solution per ISO 8407:2009

notes: Composition from Table 1 of Section 1. Corrosion rate formula provided but no numerical rate extracted for this specific alloy.

paper_title: Effect of cerium on microstructure, mechanical properties and corrosion properties of Al-Zn-Mg alloy
institution: Central South University
key_findings: Addition of trace Ce has little effect on strength but significantly improves corrosion resistance of Al-Zn-Mg alloy.
notes: Based on Section 9 abstract. No numerical composition or property data provided in the extracted text.

paper_title: Effects of Zn addition on precipitation behavior and mechanical properties of Mg-5Ga alloy
institution: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Hunan University
key_findings: Zn addition refines Mg5Ga2 rods and increases their number, accelerating aging kinetics and slightly enhancing age hardening response.
Zn segregation at the interface is responsible for the effect.
Zn addition improves strength but reduces ductility of peak-aged Mg-5Ga alloy.
notes: Based on Section 8 and Section 17. Zn content varied from 0 to 1.2 wt%. Solution treatment: 210°C×4h + 300°C×24h + 370°C×16h. Aging at 250°C. Peak-aged alloys composed of α-Mg and Mg5Ga2.

key_findings: The extruded alloy sheet with 1.01 wt% Nd demonstrates optimal combination of strength and plasticity: UTS 273 MPa, YS 214 MPa, elongation 24.2%.
More Mg24Y5 particles and Mg14Nd2Y (β) phases dispersed in the matrix with increasing Nd content.
Addition of 2.63 wt% Nd refined average grain size to the range of 4.6–1.3 μm.
notes: Data extracted from Section 2 of the provided paper content. Composition of Y and Zr not specified in the extracted text. Processing method is extrusion.

key_findings: The alloy composition is Al-5.7Zn-2.4Mg-1.4Cu-0.19Cr-0.16Fe-0.06Si (atomic %).
The alloy was solution treated at 490 °C for 1 h and then quenched to room temperature (null25 °C).
The evolution of GP zones was investigated during natural aging at room temperature.
notes: Data extracted from Section 9. Composition is given in atomic %. The paper focuses on natural aging phenomena studied via in situ TEM.

key_findings: The Mg-2.9Gd-1.5Nd-0.3Zn-0.3Zr alloy processed by equal neck angular extrusion (ECAP) achieved good comprehensive properties with UTS of 263.9 MPa, YS of 210.9 MPa, and elongation of 27.9% due to grain refinement, dislocation, precipitation of spherical β1 phase, and texture evolution.
notes: Data extracted from Section 1. The alloy was processed by equal neck angular extrusion (ECAP). The paper content mentions 'ultimate tensile strength, yield strength and elongation values of 210.9 MPa, 263.9 MPa and 27.9%', but the order in the text is ambiguous; based on typical convention, the first value (210.9 MPa) is interpreted as yield strength and the second (263.9 MPa) as ultimate tensile strength.

key_findings: Addition of 0.3 wt.% Mg improved the 0.2% yield strength of Zn from 36 MPa to 200 MPa (more than 5 times increase).
Mg addition caused a significant reduction in elongation and bendability.
notes: Data extracted from Section 2. Yield strength is 0.2% offset yield strength. Composition is based on the statement '0.3 wt.% Mg addition' to pure Zn.

key_findings: Mg addition decreased Zn peak intensity and led to η phase appearance in Al-20Zn-xMg alloys.
Elongated grains observed after hot rolling; grain diameter slightly decreased with increasing Mg content.
notes: Data extracted from Section 1. Composition is Al-20Zn-xMg, with x (Mg content) not specified in the extracted text. T6 treatment: 120°C for 17h. Strain rate for tensile test: 5e-2 s^-1.

key_findings: Extruded samples had a longer fatigue life than rolled samples due to different microstructures and crack initiation sites.
notes: Data extracted from Section 1. Only composition and qualitative fatigue behavior are provided; no numerical mechanical properties are given for this specific alloy.

key_findings: HPT sample exhibited the best mechanical performance matching among the Zn–5Ce samples with a high ultimate tensile strength of null345 MPa, yield strength of null230 MPa, a moderate elongation of null11.4%, and macro-hardness of null96.6 HB.
notes: Data extracted from Section 5. Processing includes Ce alloying followed by hot-rolling, hot-extrusion, and high-pressure torsion (HPT). Hardness is in HB scale.

key_findings: Optimum solution treatment is 470°C/60 min.
Ultimate tensile strength, yield strength and elongation of the aged studied alloys reached maximum values of 581 MPa, 561 MPa, and 10.1%, respectively.
Both tensile strength and hardness increase first and then decrease with increasing solution temperature or solution time.
Fine Al3(Sc, Zr) particles exist during aging treatment.
notes: Data extracted from Section 20. The alloy is an Al-Zn-Mg-Sc-Zr system, but exact composition (wt%) is not provided in the extracted text. The mechanical properties are for the aged condition after optimal solution treatment.

key_findings: As-cast Zn-5Ge alloy has a corrosion rate of 0.1272 mm/y.
The degradation rate of as-cast Zn-5Ge in SBF for 14 days was 0.042 mm/y.
notes: Data extracted from Section 1. Corrosion rate of 0.1272 mm/y is from a general test; the 0.042 mm/y is specifically for immersion in SBF for 14 days.

key_findings: Low-Zn/Mg alloy peak hardness: 190.3 ± 0.8 HV
Time to peak UTS: 24 h
notes: Data extracted from Section 1. Composition not specified; alloy is described as having a low Zn/Mg ratio in an Al-Zn-Mg-Cu system (implied by context). Hardness value is peak hardness.

paper_title: Effects of Zn/Gd ratio on the microstructures and mechanical properties of Mg-Zn-Gd-Zr alloys
institution: Key Lab of Electromagnetic Processing of Materials, Ministry of Education, Northeastern University, Shenyang, China

processing.casting_method: direct electrical resistance melting in mild steel crucible, cast into preheated steel mold at 200 ± 10 °C

key_findings: With Zn/Gd ratio increases from 0.27 to 1.4, second phases change from W, X and Mg5(Gd,Zn) to W, I (Mg3Zn6Gd) and MgZn2.
After extrusion, needle-like and dispersively distributed I-phase observed.
notes: Data extracted from Section 1 and Section 4. Only nominal composition for alloy GZ113 (Mg-3Zn-11Gd-0.6Zr) is provided. Mechanical properties not given in the extracted sections.

paper_title: Grain refinement mechanism and mechanical properties of wrought Zn-0.1Mg-0.02Mn alloys by rolling at different reductions
institution: College of Mechanics and Materials, Hohai University
key_findings: 80% reduction rolled Zn-0.1Mg-0.02Mn alloy showed best mechanical performance with UTS of 397.7 MPa and elongation of 15.9%.
Grain refinement mainly promoted strength enhancement with increased rolling reductions.
Basal-oriented grains refined via basal slip-induced DRX; non-basal-oriented grains refined via twinning and de-twinning.
notes: Data extracted from Section 18 and 19. Composition calculated as balance Zn (100 - 0.1 - 0.02 = 99.88 wt%). Testing temperature assumed room temperature (25°C).

key_findings: GdZn12 second phase is crushed and refined during hot rolling and extrusion
GdZn12 phase has high melting point and thermal stability
Second-phase strengthening and grain refinement improve mechanical properties
notes: Data extracted from multiple sections of the paper. Precipitate size of 5.8 ± 1.2 μm is for HR Zn-5Gd sample. Composition is nominal (Zn-5Gd).

key_findings: The most promising results were obtained in the sample subjected to 50% CR reduction and 5-min annealing at 200°C.
Ultimate tensile strength of 432 MPa, yield strength of 385 MPa, total elongation to failure of 34%.
notes: Data extracted from Section 15. Composition is nominal (Zn-3Ag-0.5Mg wt%). Processing: hot extrusion (HE) + cold rolling (CR) 50% reduction + short-time heat treatment at 200°C for 5 min.

key_findings: Alloys with Zn/Mg ratio of 6.11 owned a tensile strength of 394 MPa which represents a 38 MPa increase compared with alloys with a Zn/Mg ratio of 3.68.
Continuous grain boundary precipitate distribution in the highest Zn/Mg ratio alloy resulted in a peak intergranular corrosion depth of 52.5 µm and stress corrosion sensitivity index of 6.1%.

The intergranular corrosion depth and stress corrosion sensitivity index were 15.3 µm and 3.6%, respectively, in the alloy with a Zn/Mg ratio of 3.68 due to the coarse and discontinuous grain boundary precipitates.
notes: Data extracted from Section 14. The alloy is an Al-Zn-Mg alloy with a Zn/Mg ratio of 6.11. Exact weight percentages of Zn, Mg, and Al are not provided in the extracted text. The tensile strength value (394 MPa) is reported as ultimate tensile strength. Corrosion depth and stress corrosion sensitivity index are provided.

paper_title: Optimization of mechanical properties of dilute Mg-Zn-Y alloys prepared by rapid solidification
institution: Magnesium Research Center, Kumamoto University
key_findings: RS ribbon-consolidated Mg-Zn-Y alloys have shown exceptional mechanical properties.
Dilution and variation in metal flow rate are used for tailoring microstructure and mechanical properties.
RS ribbon-consolidation results in fine grain (700–1200 nm) microstructure.
Bimodal microstructure with a specific distribution of internal strain leads to improved mechanical properties.
notes: Grain size is estimated as the midpoint of the reported range (700–1200 nm). Composition details (Zn, Y wt%) are not provided in the extracted sections.

key_findings: The paper investigates the influence of Er/Zr micro-alloying and Zn/Mg ratio on wear resistance of Al-Zn-Mg alloys.
Total Zn+Mg content is 6 wt%.
Zn/Mg ratios designed are 2:1 and 3:1.
Alloy group with 0.1 wt% Er is designed to study effect of Er on wear performance.
High-purity aluminum, pure zinc, pure magnesium, Al-6 wt% Er, and Al-10 wt% Zr were used as raw materials.
notes: Based on limited evidence from Section 1 and Section 11. Only compositional design parameters and research objectives are extractable. No numerical mechanical or corrosion properties are provided in the given sections.

The provided paper content contains multiple sections from different studies on Zn and Al alloys, but no single complete alloy dataset with all required fields could be extracted. The content discusses various Zn-Mg, Al-Zn-Mg, and Zn-based alloys with mechanical properties mentioned in fragments, but lacks a cohesive, structured dataset for a specific alloy.

key_findings: Hot-rolled (HR) Zn-5Gd shows ultimate tensile strength of null311.6 MPa, yield strength of null236.5 MPa, and elongation of null40.6%.
HR and HE Zn-5Gd show higher corrosion resistance than pure Zn in Hanks' solution.
HR and HE Zn-5Gd show high cytocompatibility toward MC3T3-E1 and MG-63 cells, good antibacterial effects, and better in vitro osteogenic activity.
HE Zn-5Gd exhibits better in vivo biocompatibility, osteogenesis, and osteointegration than pure Zn and pure Ti.
notes: Data extracted from Section 15. The alloy is hot-rolled (HR) Zn-5Gd. Composition is inferred from the alloy name (Zn-5Gd, likely 5 wt% Gd, balance Zn). Mechanical properties are explicitly stated. Processing details (hot rolling) are mentioned but no parameters given.

paper_title: Effects of Zn/Gd ratio on the microstructures and mechanical properties of Mg-Zn-Gd-Zr alloys
institution: Northeastern University, China
key_findings: With Zn/Gd ratio increase from 0.27 to 1.4, second phases change from W, X, Mg5(Gd,Zn) to W, I (Mg3Zn6Gd), MgZn2.
After extrusion, needle-like and dispersively distributed I-phase observed.
notes: Based on Section 1 only. Zn/Gd mass ratio of 0.27 corresponds to first alloy. Numerical mechanical properties not provided in extracted sections.

key_findings: The ultimate tensile strength of as-extruded Zn-Mg binary alloys increased from 250 to 340 MPa, but the elongation dramatically decreased from 22% to 6% with increasing Mg content from 0.15 to 1 wt%, which is attributed to the collective role of grain refinement and second phase (Mg2Zn11).
notes: Based on limited evidence from Section 1. The paper content discusses Zn-Mg binary alloys but does not provide a specific alloy ID, year, authors, or full composition. The mechanical properties are reported as ranges for Mg content varying from 0.15 to 1 wt%, not for a single alloy. The alloy system is Zn-Mg, and the second phase identified is Mg2Zn11.

key_findings: Pure Zn prepared by casting has yield strength of about 20 MPa, elongation at break of 0.25%, ultimate tensile strength of about 50 MPa, and hardness of about 37 HV.
Mechanical properties of pure Zn are lower than the standard for vascular support based on human body fixed materials.
notes: Data extracted from Section 1 of the provided paper content. Only pure Zn data was fully extractable; other alloys mentioned lack complete numerical property data in the given text.

key_findings: Filler metal with high Mg and low Mn proportion can enhance strength of Al-Zn-Mg alloys.
T73 heat treatment enhanced fusion zone strength of weldments made with ER5356.
AA7003 and AA7005 after T6 heat treatment showed the highest UTS.
Linear relationship exists between UTS and hardness for AA7005 (hardness above 50 HV).
notes: Data extracted from Section 1. Specific numerical values for UTS, YS, elongation, and hardness are not provided in the text for AA7003/AA7005 T6 condition.

key_findings: Zn-0.5Mg-0.05Fe alloy stored at 25°C shows tensile stress of 155.4 ± 9.6 MPa and elongation 18.2 ± 0.7% at 0 days; after half year: 170.3 ± 5.8 MPa, 13.1 ± 0.3%; after 1 year: 185.7 ± 7.3 MPa, 11.3 ± 0.4%.
notes: Data extracted from Section 11, Table 2. Only tensile stress (not UTS/YS) and elongation provided for storage conditions. Composition is nominal wt%.

key_findings: Rolled Zn-0.5%Li-0.1%Ag alloy shows elastic modulus of 83.1 GPa and tensile strength of 251.6 MPa.
Extrusion process improved elastic modulus (92.8 GPa) and tensile strength (378.5 MPa) compared to as-cast state.
Rolling decreased both elastic modulus and tensile strength compared to extruded state.
notes: Data extracted from Section 12. Composition calculated as balance Zn (100 - 0.5 - 0.1 = 99.4 wt%). Strain rate is 0.001/s for mechanical testing.

key_findings: Precipitation strengthening is the dominant mechanism in Al-Zn-Mg-Cu alloys.
The alloy with a Zn/Mg ratio of 1.30 showed the highest precipitation strengthening contribution of 496 MPa.
As the Zn/Mg ratio increased from 0.53 to 2.51, the fraction of T' phase decreased from 83.95%.
The total Δσppt for five alloys with various Zn/Mg ratios were 406, 452, 496, 466, and 419 MPa.
notes: Data extracted from multiple sections of the paper. The alloy with Zn/Mg ratio 1.30 exhibited the highest precipitation strengthening increment (496 MPa). Aging temperature is 120°C based on Section 10. The paper discusses Al-Zn-Mg-Cu alloys, but specific composition weight percentages are not provided in the extracted text.

key_findings: 12p-ECAP Zn-Mg-Y alloy shows highest strength (465 MPa UTS) together with good ductility (11% elongation).
Increasing ECAP number from 4 to 8 increases strength but barely changes ductility.
12p-ECAP alloy exhibits work-hardening behavior with dσ/dε > 0 until true strain reaches 11%.
Serration in stress-strain curve suggests twinning might happen in 12p-ECAP alloy.
notes: Data extracted from Section 1 and Section 19. Composition details (Zn, Mg, Y wt%) not provided in the given text. Yield strength (YS) for 12p-ECAP alloy is not explicitly given; the text mentions 'TYS changed little compared with that of 8p-ECAP alloy' but no numerical value for 12p-ECAP YS is provided. The UTS of 465 MPa and elongation of 11% are explicitly stated for the 12p-ECAP alloy.

key_findings: Multi-pass equal channel angular pressed (ECAPed) Zn-Mg-Mn alloy achieved UTS of 358 MPa and elongation of 23.5%.
notes: Data extracted from Section 5. The alloy is described as 'Zn-Mg-Mn' in the text but the section heading and figure caption refer to 'Zn-Mg-Y'. Based on the context, the mechanical properties are for an ECAPed Zn-Mg-Mn alloy. Composition details (wt%) are not provided in the extracted text.

microstructure.texture: basal texture enhanced by unDRXed grains

key_findings: 0.5% Ce/La addition decreases grain size and enhances grain boundary strengthening, improving TYS.
1.0% Ce/La addition replaces Ca2Mg6Zn3 phases with T10 phases.
Increased volume fraction of unDRXed grains enhances basal texture, providing strengthening effect.
notes: Data extracted from Section 1 of the provided paper content. Composition is nullimate (0.5Ce/La). EIS data provided but not fully extracted due to incomplete table.

key_findings: Hot-extruded Zn-0.4Li alloy has a UTS of 405.3 MPa and an EL of 27.4%.
Higher Li content generates more β-LiZn4 particles which facilitate DRX through particle stimulated nucleation.
notes: Data extracted from Section 2. Composition is based on wt% Li. Processing method is hot extrusion.

key_findings: Zn/HA composite showed higher corrosion rate (0.41 mm/year) compared to pure SPS Zinc (0.26 mm/year) based on weight loss measurement.
Weight gains were significantly higher for Zn/HA composite (2.5%) than for pure zinc (0.5%), confirming higher corrosion rate.
notes: Data extracted from Section 1, Table 4. Corrosion rate for SPS Zn/HA16 composite is 0.41 mm/year. Pure SPS Zinc corrosion rate is 0.26 mm/year.

key_findings: The alloy exhibits dynamic recrystallization (DRX) during room temperature compression.
Work-hardening dominates at the beginning of compression, followed by DRX-induced softening.
Rheological stress shows jagged fluctuations with increasing strain rates at room temperature due to discontinuities from DRX events.
notes: Data extracted from Section 1. Only qualitative mechanical behavior during compression is described; no numerical mechanical properties are provided. Testing temperature is room temperature (assumed null25°C).

key_findings: Zn-1Cu-0.5Ag shows UTS of 332.90 MPa, YS of 318.70 MPa, and elongation of 26.32%.
Vickers hardness of Zn-1Cu-0.5Ag is 101.9 HV.
Ductile fracture morphology with deep dimples observed.
notes: Data extracted from Section 8. Alloy is as-extruded. Composition is in wt%.

key_findings: Hot-rolled Zn-5Ge alloy shows significant enhancement in mechanical properties compared to pure as-cast Zn, with ductility of 21.6%, yield strength of 175.1 MPa, and ultimate tensile strength of 237 MPa.
The corrosion rate of hot-rolled Zn-5Ge is 0.2255 mm/y, lower than hot-rolled pure Zn (0.3057 mm/y).
notes: Data extracted from Section 10 and Section 13. The alloy is hot-rolled. Composition is based on 'Zn-5Ge' which is interpreted as 5 wt% Ge, balance Zn.

paper_title: Influence of microalloying and homogenization on the microstructures and mechanical properties of as-extruded Zn alloys
institution: Key Laboratory of Electromagnetic Processing of Materials, Ministry of Education, Northeastern University, Shenyang 110819, PR China
key_findings: Microalloying and homogenization can effectively enhance the mechanical properties (notably plasticity) of Zn alloys by controlling the formation and precipitation of second phases.
Elevating the homogenization temperature markedly improved ductility; HT390/ET170 alloy exhibited an elongation of 133 %, nearly double that of the HT330/ET170 alloy (71 %).
This enhancement is attributed to finer grains and a higher density of submicron particles, which facilitated grain boundary sliding (GBS) and DRX during tensile deformation.
notes: Based on limited evidence from Section 4 and Section 6. The alloy composition is inferred from the paper's description of Zn-0.2Cu-0.2Mn based alloys with 0.2 wt% Al addition. Specific numerical mechanical properties for this exact alloy are not provided in the extracted text; only comparative elongation values for different homogenization treatments are given.

paper_title: Stability of damping capacity and mechanical properties of high-Zn-concentration Al–Zn–Mg–Sc alloy
institution: Key Laboratory of New Processing Technology for Nonferrous Metal & Materials, Ministry of Education, Guilin University of Technology, Guilin 541004, China
key_findings: Decreasing Zn content led to disappearance of Zn phases and decrease of intragranular η phases density and solution atom number.
Increasing Mg content did not increase the number of solution atoms.
notes: Based on Section 15. Composition is nullimate from alloy designation Al–20Zn–0.5Mg–0.5Sc. Other elements (Al) are balance but not explicitly stated as numerical value.

key_findings: Superplastic deformation of Zn-0.033 Mg alloy exhibits strong dependence on deformation temperature.
Ultrafine-grained Zn-0.033 Mg alloy achieves superplastic elongation of 376 ± 79% at 453 K and 0.001 s−1.
Grain boundary sliding is the dominant superplastic deformation mechanism, accommodated by {1012} tensile twins.
notes: Elongation value is 376 ± 79% (superplastic elongation). Data extracted from Section 1. Composition is nullimate (balance Zn).

key_findings: ZTM641−2Al alloy has the best mechanical properties at 200°C: UTS 105 MPa, YS 93 MPa, EL 74%.
Al addition leads to formation of high-melting second phases Al8Mn5 and Mg2Sn.
Excessive Al content causes aggregation of second phases at grain boundaries, reducing elevated temperature strength.
notes: Data extracted from Section 11. Composition of base alloy ZTM641 (Mg-Zn-Sn-Mn) not fully specified; only Al content (2 wt%) is given. Processing: solution treated at 430°C for 2h, water cooled, then single aged at 180°C for 12h. Mechanical properties tested at 200°C.

key_findings: Zn-0.08wt.%Mg alloy processed by conventional extrusion and subsequent drawing achieved yield strength of 221 MPa, ultimate tensile strength of 339 MPa, and elongation at break of 40%.
The alloy had the problem of age hardening and strain rate sensitivity.
notes: Data extracted from Section 11. Composition calculated as balance Zn with 0.08 wt% Mg. Processing details: conventional extrusion and subsequent drawing.

key_findings: The average strain rate sensitivity coefficient (m) values for RRA Al-12Zn-2.4Mg-1.1Cu alloys are 0.017 (2 mm), 0.013 (3 mm), 0.011 (4 mm), and 0.012 (5 mm).
Positive n and m values indicate tensile strength increases with elongation when strain rate is increased.
Thicker samples contain more Zn elements to form strengthening phases.
notes: Data extracted from Section 1 and Section 14. The alloy is referred to as RRA Al-12Zn-2.4Mg-1.1Cu. Specific mechanical property values (UTS, YS, elongation) are not provided in the extracted text. The n value for 2 mm thick sample is mentioned as 0.81-0.84 in Section 14.

key_findings: After extrusion and rolling, the elastic modulus and tensile strength of Zn-0.5%Li-0.1%Ag alloy decreased to 83.1 GPa and 251.6 MPa, respectively.
notes: Data extracted from Section 11. The alloy was processed by extrusion followed by rolling. The as-cast and extruded states also have reported properties in the same section.

paper_title: Optimization of mechanical properties of dilute Mg-Zn-Y alloys prepared by rapid solidification
institution: Magnesium Research Center, Kumamoto University; Department of Physics of Materials, Charles University; Nuclear Physics Institute of the Czech Academy of Science; Graduated Student, Department of Materials Science, Kumamoto University
key_findings: RS ribbon-consolidated Mg-Zn-Y alloys have shown exceptional mechanical properties.
Dilution and variation in metal flow rate are used for tailoring microstructure and mechanical properties.
RS ribbon-consolidation results in fine grain (700–1200 nm) microstructure.
Bimodal microstructure with a specific distribution of internal strain leads to improved mechanical properties.
notes: Grain size is estimated as the midpoint of the reported range 700–1200 nm. Composition details (Zn, Mg, Y wt%) are not provided in the extracted sections.

key_findings: Addition of 0.05 and 0.09 wt% Al enhanced undercooling of Zn-25Sn-XAl alloy, promoting nucleation of liquid solder.
notes: Based on limited evidence from Section 1. The paper discusses Zn-25Sn-XAl (X=0, 0.01, 0.09 wt%) but only mentions 0.05 and 0.09 wt% Al in the conclusion. No specific mechanical properties for this exact composition were provided in the extracted text.

paper_title: Microstructure and mechanical properties of the micrograined hypoeutectic Zn–Mg alloy
institution: University of Chemistry and Technology Prague
key_findings: The alloy was prepared by gravity casting, hot extrusion, and a combination of rapid solidification and hot extrusion.
The study characterized the alloy using light microscopy, SEM, TEM, and XRD.
Vickers hardness, tensile, and compressive tests were performed.
notes: Based on limited evidence from Section 15. The paper abstract mentions three processing routes but only the gravity casting method is explicitly named in the extracted text. Mechanical property values are not provided in the extracted snippet.

key_findings: GdZn12 second phase is crushed into finer particles (5.8 ± 1.2 μm) after hot rolling (HR) and distributed along rolling direction.
In hot extruded (HE) Zn-5Gd, GdZn12 second phase size is 7.4 ± 2.7 μm, showing a streamlined structure.
Heat deformation treatment induces complete recrystallization of pure Zn and refines α-Zn grains and GdZn12 phases.
notes: Data extracted from Section 1 and Section 9. Precipitate size (5.8 μm) is from HR Zn-5Gd sample. Composition is based on Zn-5Gd alloy mentioned in Section 9.

Based on Section 1: Zn-Al ingots homogeneously annealed at 340°C for 3 hrs. Solid solubility of Al in Zn at this temperature is null1.0 wt%. Tensile test at room temperature, crosshead speed 0.5 mm/s (strain rate calculated as 0.5 mm/s / 12.5 mm = 0.04 /s). Gauge length 12.5 mm, diameter 6.0 mm. 0.2% proof stress used as yield strength. Hardness test mentioned but no values given. No specific mechanical property values (UTS, YS, elongation, hardness) are provided in the extracted text.

key_findings: Superplastic deformation of Zn-0.033Mg alloy shows strong dependence on deformation temperature.
Ultrafine-grained Zn-0.033Mg alloy achieves superplastic elongation of 376 ± 79% at 453 K and 0.001 s⁻¹.
Grain boundary sliding is the dominant superplastic deformation mechanism, accommodated by {1012} tensile twins.
notes: Data extracted from Section 1. Elongation value is 376 ± 79% (mean ± standard deviation). Testing temperature is 453 K (180°C). Strain rate is 0.001 s⁻¹. Alloy is described as ultrafine-grained (UFG).

key_findings: Hot compression deformation of Zn-0.5Mg-0.05Fe alloy prepared by powder metallurgy exhibits three phases: work hardening, dynamic softening, and steady-state fluidization.
Increasing deformation temperature and decreasing strain rate reduce rheological stress.
The intrinsic equation for hot compression is derived as ε̇ = 1.334 × 10^10 × [sinh(0.0053σ)]^7.62947 × exp(-109.931691/RT).
Dynamic recrystallization (DRX) occurs during hot compression, with mechanisms including DDRX and CDRX.
At room temperature, work-hardening dominates initially, followed by DRX softening leading to jagged fluctuations in rheological stress.
notes: Data extracted from Sections 1 and 5. The paper focuses on hot compression behavior of powder metallurgy Zn-0.5Mg-0.05Fe alloy. Mechanical properties (UTS, YS, elongation) are not provided for this specific alloy in the given text. Year, title, DOI, authors, and institution are not available from the provided content.

key_findings: HR Zn-5Gd showed ultimate tensile strength of null311.6 MPa, yield strength of null236.5 MPa, and elongation of null40.6%.
HE Zn-5Gd showed ultimate tensile strength of null326.5 MPa, yield strength of null153.0 MPa, and elongation of null57.6%.

The alloy had high strength and ductility, appropriate corrosion and degradation rates, effective antibacterial ability, high in vitro cytocompatibility, and in vivo osteogenesis and osteointegration ability.
notes: Data extracted from Section 12. The paper discusses Zn-5Gd alloy fabricated by casting and thermally processed by hot rolling (HR) and hot extrusion (HE). Mechanical properties for HR condition are listed; HE condition values are also provided in findings. Year is inferred from the journal availability date (2024).

key_findings: Zn-5Mg showed the highest strength of 335.4 MPa, higher than other fabrication methods.
Tensile strength first increased then decreased with increasing WE43 content.
Elongation decreased with increasing WE43 content.
notes: Data extracted from Section 3. Composition of WE43 not specified. Processing method is L-PBF (laser powder bed fusion) based on context.

key_findings: Pure Zn prepared by casting has yield strength of about 20 MPa, elongation at break of 0.25%, ultimate tensile strength of about 50 MPa, and hardness of about 37 HV.
Mechanical properties of pure Zn are lower than the standard for vascular support based on human body fixed materials.
notes: Data extracted from Section 1. The paper discusses pure Zn as a baseline for comparison with Zn alloys.

paper_title: Stability of damping capacity and mechanical properties of high-Zn-concentration Al–Zn–Mg–Sc alloy
institution: Key Laboratory of New Processing Technology for Nonferrous Metal & Materials, Ministry of Education, Guilin University of Technology, Guilin 541004, China
key_findings: Al–20Zn–0.5 Mg–0.5Sc alloy contained fine grains, a high number of solution atoms, Zn phases, Al3Sc phases, and high-density fine η and η' phases.
Decreasing Zn content led to disappearance of Zn phases and decrease of intragranular η phases density and solution atom number.
Increasing Mg content did not increase the number of solution atoms.
notes: Data extracted from Sections 8 and 11. Composition is in wt%. The paper focuses on damping capacity and mechanical property stability. Specific numerical mechanical properties for this alloy are not provided in the extracted sections.

key_findings: Superplastic elongation of 376 ± 79% achieved at 453 K and 0.001 s⁻¹ for Zn-0.033Mg alloy with grain size of 1.53 μm.
Grain boundary sliding is the dominant superplastic deformation mechanism, accommodated by {1012} tensile twins.
Superplastic deformation shows strong dependence on deformation temperature; fine-grained specimens exhibit transition from continuous to discontinuous yielding with increasing temperature.
Decreasing grain size reduces the temperature needed for superplastic elongation, minimizing grain growth and maintaining thermal stability.
notes: Data extracted from multiple sections (Section 1, Section 16, Section 19). Elongation value is the reported superplastic elongation (376%) with standard deviation (±79%). Grain size of 1.53 μm is from Section 19. Composition Zn-0.033Mg is in wt%.

key_findings: Alloying Cu significantly improved the ultimate tensile strength of Zn.
Zn-2Cu exhibited great cytocompatibility.
The study advanced the use of Zn-2Cu alloy for treating bone defects.
notes: Data extracted from Section 1. The paper mentions Zn-2Cu alloy prepared via laser-powder-bed-fusion (L-PBF) additive manufacturing, but specific numerical mechanical properties for this alloy are not provided in the given text.

paper_title: Effect of Zn/Mg ratio on precipitation and strengthening of Al–Zn–Mg alloys after aging
institution: Central South University
key_findings: The influence of Zn/Mg ratio (1.2-3.5) on the precipitation and strengthening effect of Al–Zn–Mg (Zn + Mg = 10 wt%) alloys aged at 130 °C was investigated.
The hardness of the peak-aged alloys tends to increase with the increase of Zn/Mg ratio.
notes: Based on Section 12. The paper title and authors are extracted from the section header. The Zn/Mg ratio range and aging temperature are explicitly stated. The total Zn+Mg content is 10 wt%. Specific numerical mechanical properties are not provided in this section.

key_findings: The as-cast Zn-0.4Mn-0.8Li alloy exhibits optimal mechanical properties with TYS of 203.2 ± 8.9 MPa and UTS of 271.5 ± 20.0 MPa, nullimately 4-fold compared with pure Zn.
notes: Data extracted from Section 12. The alloy is as-cast. UTS and TYS values are given as mean ± standard deviation. Elongation not explicitly provided for this specific composition in the extracted text.

Data extracted from Section 1. Hardness is Brinell. Compressive strength value is the higher of the two compressive values listed (156.98 ± 7.59). Strain rate is 2 × 10^-4 (units assumed to be s^-1 based on context).

key_findings: Mechanical properties of Zn-0.5Mg-0.05Fe alloys stored at 25°C for 0 days.
notes: Data extracted from Table 2 of Section 12. The alloy was stored at 25°C for 0 days. Tensile stress is reported as 155.4 ± 9.6 MPa, elongation as 18.2 ± 0.7%.

key_findings: Both ultimate tensile strength and elongation of Zn-Mn alloys increase due to minor Mn additions.
notes: Based on Section 1: The paper discusses as-extruded Zn-Mn alloys with Mn contents of 0.2, 0.4, 0.6 wt%. Specific mechanical property values for Zn-0.2Mn are not provided in the extracted text, only trends. Pure Zn UTS=117 MPa, Elongation=14%.

key_findings: Solution treated at 743 K for 1 h, then water-quenched.
notes: Composition from Table 1, Alloy A. Processing details from Section 2. No mechanical properties directly linked to this specific alloy in the provided text.

paper_title: Materials Science & Engineering A 677 (2016) 384–392
key_findings: The addition of Al enhanced the undercooling of Zn-25Sn-XAl alloy, promoting nucleation of liquid solder.
ZS-XAl alloys exhibit good mechanical properties and oxidation resistance compared to high lead content alloys.
Long-term reliability of Zn-25Sn-XAl alloy may need further investigation due to Al segregation at grain boundaries.
notes: Data extracted from Section 1 and Section 17. UTS and elongation values are the maximum reported for as-cast ZS-XAl (X=0–0.09%) at strain rate 8.3e-4 s^-1. Composition based on Zn-25Sn-XAl with X=0.09 wt% Al. Year inferred from DOI and journal volume.

paper_title: Microstructure refinement, mechanical performances and degradability of biomedical Zn-2Cu alloy by ultrasonic melting and homogenization treatment
institution: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Lanzhou University of Technology, Lanzhou 730050, China; Lanzhou University Second Hospital, Lanzhou 730050, China
key_findings: Ultrasonic melt treatment (UMT) refined coarse α-Zn grains into fine equiaxed grains.
Mean grain size decreased from 133.4 μm to 65.3 μm when ultrasound power was enhanced.
CuZn5 phase was refined from coarse block to finer morphology.
notes: Based on Section 1 only. Ultrasonic powers tested: 0 W, 300 W, 600 W, 900 W. Grain size of 65.3 μm corresponds to highest power (900 W). Mechanical and corrosion data not provided in extracted section.

key_findings: Maximum tensile strain of 236.2% achieved at ambient temperature and 0.0001 s⁻¹ strain rate.
Grain size of MnZn13 declined from 0.27 to 0.07 mm with increase in deformation ratio.
Ultra-fine grains with negligible textures improve ductility by lowering mechanical anisotropy.
Superplasticity observed with UTS of only 106 MPa.
notes: Data extracted from Section 1 and Section 7. Grain size of 0.27 mm is for Zn grains at 95.8% deformation ratio; at 99.5% deformation ratio, Zn grains are 0.27-0.35 mm and MnZn13 is 0.07 mm. UTS of 106 MPa corresponds to the finest grain condition exhibiting superplasticity.

paper_title: Fabrication, Microstructures, and Mechanical Properties of Zn-0.1Mg-1Mn (wt.%) Alloy Tube
key_findings: The alloy was extruded into tubes at temperatures of 100, 200, and 300 °C.
Grains were refined to sizes of 1.0, 1.5, and 1.9 μm at extrusion temperatures of 100, 200, and 300 °C, respectively.
Precipitates arranged along the extrusion direction at 100 °C and became randomly dispersed with increasing temperature.
The alloy extruded at 100 °C exhibited excellent properties.
notes: Based on Section 14. The paper title and authors are extracted from the section header. Grain size of 1.0 μm corresponds to extrusion at 100 °C. Exact mechanical property values (UTS, YS, elongation) for this specific alloy are not provided in the extracted text, only described as 'excellent'.

key_findings: Yield strength and ultimate tensile strength reach 241.2 ± 4.7 MPa and 265.8 ± 1.3 MPa, showing 294.4% and 230.3% improvement over pure Zn.
Strength improvement is mainly derived from grain refinement strengthening and Orowan looping strengthening.
Improved strength achieved without sacrificing ductility.
notes: Data extracted from Section 16. The alloy was fabricated via controlled stir casting and hot extrusion. The heterostructure consists of ununiformly dispersing CuZn5 second phase in the Zn matrix.

key_findings: Primary α-Zn morphology changed from coarse columnar crystals to equiaxed grains with increasing ultrasonic power.
Average primary trunk length was 71.52 μm, average width was 8.62 μm.
Precipitated second phase mean size was close to 37.08 μm.
With ultrasonic treatment, primary α-Zn size decreased to 47.25 μm.
notes: Data extracted from Section 2. Composition is in wt%. Grain size and precipitate size are from the alloy without ultrasonic treatment (Zn-0 alloy) unless specified otherwise.

key_findings: Sc and Zr co-addition increases tensile strength and elongation slightly after T6 aging.
Al3(Sc,Zr) precipitates influence microstructural evolution and corrosion resistance.
notes: Data extracted from Section 1 and Section 16. Alloy A is the base Al-Zn-Mg alloy without Sc/Zr. Composition details are referenced to Table 1 which is not provided. Aging treatment: T6 at 120°C for 24h.

paper_title: Anticoagulation Degradation behavior Mechanical properties Zn-based biodegradable metals Zn–Mg–RE alloys
institution: Centre for Advanced Materials Processing and Manufacturing (AMPAM), The University of Queensland, Brisbane, Queensland 4072, Australia
key_findings: Alloying efficacy of rare earth elements (Er, Dy, Ho) on microstructure, mechanical properties, corrosion and wear behavior, and in vitro biological properties of Zn–1Mg–0.1RE alloys was studied.
notes: Based on Section 10. Only composition and general research focus are available; no specific numerical mechanical or corrosion data were provided in the extracted text.

paper_title: Biodegradable Zn-Mg-Ce alloys with good mechanical properties, degradability, and cytocompatibility matching for potential load-bearing bone-implant applications
institution: Wenzhou Medical University
key_findings: The alloy is abbreviated as ZM-0.1Li.
The paper discusses biodegradable Zn-Mg-Ce alloys for bone-implant applications.
notes: Based on limited evidence, the composition is inferred from the abbreviation ZM-0.1Li defined in Section 2, where ZM is Zn-0.1Al-0.1Mn-0.1Cu-0.1Ag. The paper title and authors are from Section 4, but the specific alloy data for this composition is not provided in the extracted text.

corrosion_properties.corrosion_test_method: Electrochemical test (polarization curves) and immersion test in simulated body fluid at 37°C

key_findings: The strength of Zn-2Cu-0.8Mg-0.1Sr-0.1Gd alloy is slightly lower than that of Zn-2Cu-0.8Mg-0.2Gd alloy, possibly due to lower content of Mg2Zn11 + Zn.
Coarse and sharp-angled SrZn13 compounds cause stress concentration during tension, reducing elongation and strength.
notes: Corrosion rate from electrochemical test is 32.82 µm/year; from immersion test is 0.223 mm/year. Data extracted from Section 2 of the provided content.

key_findings: Samples with titanium addition showed better mechanical properties than the base material ZL2.
UTS ranges from 270 MPa (0.05 wt.% Ti) to 264 MPa (0.33 wt.% Ti).
YS ranges from 236 MPa (0.05 wt.% Ti) to 250 MPa (0.33 wt.% Ti).
Base ZL2 alloy has UTS of 200 MPa and YS of 200 MPa.
notes: Data extracted from Section 2. The base alloy is ZL2. Only the 0.05 wt% Ti variant is fully extracted here; other Ti contents are noted in findings.

key_findings: Peak hardness of 190.7 HV achieved after 36 h of aging.
Main precipitate at peak hardness is η' phase, which is the main strengthening phase.
Coarsening of η' phase and transformation to η phase causes a slow decline in hardness after peak.
notes: Data extracted from Section 6. The alloy is an Al-Zn-Mg alloy aged at an unspecified temperature (likely 120°C based on context from other sections, but not explicitly stated for this specific data point).

key_findings: The highest ultimate tensile strength of 259.4 MPa and yield strength of 234.8 MPa were achieved in the HR Zn–1Mg–0.1Ho, together with elongation of 16.8% and hardness of 88.6 HV.
notes: Data extracted from Section 5. Alloy is hot-rolled (HR). Composition is nullimate (balance Zn).

corrosion_properties.corrosion_test_method: electrochemical polarization testing in Hank's solution

key_findings: Ultrasonic power of 600 W refined primary α-Zn to 43.63 μm and changed SrZn13 morphology from irregular polygonal to small block-like.
UTS improved by 39.4% (from 71 to 99 MPa) and EL increased by 396% (from 0.25% to 1.24%) at 600 W USV compared to without USV.
Corrosion rate at 600 W USV was 7.438 mm/year in Hank's solution.
notes: Data extracted from Section 8 and Section 12. Composition is nullimate balance Zn. Grain size and mechanical properties correspond to the 600 W ultrasonic power condition. Corrosion rate is for the 600 W condition.

key_findings: Initial dislocations generated during loading significantly change the stress relaxation behaviour of the material.
Dislocation creep mechanism plays the dominant role in stress relaxation of the alloy at the investigated stresses and temperatures, with the stress exponent n ranging from 3 to 8.
notes: Based on Section 3 and Section 9. The alloy AA7B04-P composition is Al-5.97Zn-2.48Mg-1.51Cu (wt%). The paper investigates stress relaxation ageing (SRA) behaviour. No specific mechanical property values (UTS, YS, elongation) for this alloy were provided in the extracted sections.

key_findings: Hot-extruded Zn-0.4Li alloy has a UTS of 405.3 MPa and an EL of 27.4%.
Higher Li content generates more β-LiZn4 particles which facilitate DRX through particle stimulated nucleation.
notes: Data extracted from Section 2. Composition is based on Li content of 0.4% (assumed wt%).

paper_title: Effect of heat treatments on the microstructure and mechanical properties of Zn-15 wt% Al alloy
institution: Zaozhuang University; Shaoxing Tianlong Tin Materials Co., Ltd
key_findings: After being annealed below 275 °C, granular α particles were distributed homogeneously in the η phase matrix.
A lamellar microstructure along with isolated η grains was observed when the alloys were annealed above 275 °C, followed by air cooling.
notes: Data extracted from Section 16. Only composition and annealing temperature are explicitly provided; mechanical properties are mentioned in the abstract but not numerically reported in the provided excerpt.

key_findings: The coarse blocky phases are rich in Sr and Zn, identified as SrZn13 phase.
Fine particles contain almost exclusively Sn element, identified as Sn phase.
SrZn13 phase first precipitates from the Zn alloy at 420°C.
Sn phase precipitates through eutectic reaction at 232°C.
Microstructure refinement is attributed to the high melting point of SrZn13.
notes: Data extracted from Section 1 and Section 14. Composition is inferred from alloy name Zn–2Sn–0.5Sr. Phase identification confirmed by EDS and XRD.

paper_title: Effects of trace amounts of Zr and Sc on the recrystallization behavior and mechanical properties of Al-4.5Zn-1.6Mg alloys
institution: National Defense University, Taiwan; National Central University, Taiwan; National Chung-Shan Institute of Science and Technology, Taiwan
key_findings: Addition of 0.1% Zr to Al-4.5Zn-1.6Mg alloy inhibits recrystallization and refines grain structure.
Co-addition of Sc and Zr further improves mechanical properties and corrosion resistance via Al3(Sc,Zr) precipitates.
notes: Based on Section 13 and Section 1. Composition of Al-4.5Zn-1.6Mg is nominal; Zr addition is 0.1 wt% as stated; Sc addition amount not specified in extracted text but implied as trace. Mechanical and corrosion data not fully extractable from provided sections.

key_findings: Coupling modification improved tensile strength by 77.15% and elongation by 112.68% for ZSCB high-temperature solder.
notes: Based on limited evidence from Section 1. The paper mentions ZSCB solder with melting point 393.4°C, but exact composition (wt%) and mechanical property absolute values are not provided in the extracted text. Only percentage improvements are given.

paper_title: Influence of microalloying and homogenization on the microstructures and mechanical properties of as-extruded Zn alloys
institution: Northeastern University
key_findings: The as-extruded Zn-0.1Al-0.1Mn-0.1Cu-0.1Ag alloy exhibited significantly enhanced mechanical properties compared to pure Zn, with elongation of 82.2 ± 2.94%, YS of 154 ± 1.7 MPa, and UTS of 207 ± 0.9 MPa.
notes: Data extracted from Section 6. The paper discusses the synergistic effects of Al addition and homogenization temperature on Zn-0.2Cu-0.2Mn based alloys, but the specific composition and properties for Zn-0.1Al-0.1Mn-0.1Cu-0.1Ag are from prior research referenced in the same section.

key_findings: Pure Zn prepared by casting has yield strength of about 20 MPa, elongation at break of 0.25%, ultimate tensile strength of about 50 MPa, and hardness of about 37 HV. These mechanical properties are lower than the standard for vascular support based on human body fixed materials.
notes: Data extracted from Section 1 of the provided paper content. The values are for pure Zn prepared by casting.

key_findings: Two-stage homogenization heat treatment improved T6 hardness, yield strength, and tensile strength compared to one-stage homogenization, but decreased ductility slightly.
notes: Data extracted from Section 6, Table 3 for alloy B (0.05Sc) after two-stage homogenization and T6 aging (120°C/24h). Composition is Al-Zn-Mg with trace Sc and Zr; exact Zn, Mg, Al wt% not provided in the extracted text.

paper_title: Microstructure, mechanical properties and in vitro degradation properties of Zn–Fe alloys
institution: China University of Petroleum (East China)

FeZn13 phase observed

paper_title: Microstructure, mechanical properties and in vitro degradation properties of Zn–Fe alloys
institution: School of Materials Science and Engineering, China University of Petroleum (East China)
key_findings: FeZn13 phase is hard (270 HV) and brittle, separates the matrix, and reduces strength and ductility.
Addition of Fe refines grain size (Zn-3Cu-1Fe: 2.17 μm vs Zn-3Cu: 4.30 μm).
Hardness of Zn-3Cu-1Fe is about 23% higher than Zn-3Cu.
Yield strength and tensile strength of Zn-3Cu-1Fe decreased by 11% and 6% respectively compared to Zn-3Cu.
Elongation of Zn-3Cu-1Fe decreased by 57.30% compared to Zn-3Cu.
Fracture surface of Zn-3Cu-1Fe shows cleavage fracture; cracks initiate at FeZn13/matrix interface.
notes: Data extracted from multiple sections of the paper. Composition is nominal (Zn-3Cu-1Fe wt%). Grain size is for extruded condition. Mechanical property changes are relative to Zn-3Cu alloy; absolute values not provided in the extracted text.

key_findings: T4 treatment (low-temperature at 60°C for 10h) yields highest tensile strength (558.8 MPa) but lowest elongation (1.7%).
T6 treatment (90°C/10h + 140°C/24h) reduces strength to 544.7 MPa but increases elongation to 2.2%.
T6I4 treatment further reduces strength to 536.8 MPa and increases elongation to 2.5%.
GP zones transform into spherical η phases during T6 aging.
T6I4 aging results in elongated η phases and clean grain boundaries.
notes: Data extracted from multiple sections of the provided paper content. Composition is nominal for EN AW-7021 alloy. Mechanical properties for T4 state are reported. Corrosion potential for T4 state is -0.75 V. Aging treatment details are from Section 1.

key_findings: Hot-rolled Zn-5Ge alloy degradation rate in SBF is 0.2255 mm/y.
notes: Data extracted from Section 1. Composition is inferred as Zn-5Ge (wt%) based on 'Zne5Ge' notation.

key_findings: First aging peak results from high-density GP zone (especially GP II zones)
Metastable phases formed at intermediate aging stage, semi-coherent with matrix, effectively resist dislocation movement
Reasonable agreement between calculated and experimental precipitation hardening found at 120 °C
notes: Based on Section 1 evidence; paper discusses modeling of precipitation hardening in Al-Zn-Mg alloys at 120°C aging temperature. Specific composition and numerical mechanical properties not provided in this section.

paper_title: Microstructure, mechanical properties and in vitro degradation properties of Zn–Fe alloys
