notes: Based on evidence from Sections 6, 9, and 20. Fe content varied (0, 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2 wt%). Specific mechanical property values not provided in extracted text.

paper_title: Microstructure refinement, mechanical performances and degradability of biomedical Zn-2Cu alloy by ultrasonic melting and homogenization treatment
institution: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Lanzhou University of Technology
key_findings: Ultrasonic melt treatment (UMT) refined coarse α-Zn grains into fine equiaxed grains.
Mean grain size decreased from 133.4 μm to 65.3 μm when ultrasound power was enhanced.
CuZn5 precipitating phase was significantly refined from coarse block morphology.
notes: Based on evidence from Section 1 only. Grain size of 65.3 μm corresponds to 900 W UMT condition. Composition is nominal Zn-2Cu (wt%).

key_findings: h-MgZn2 phase identified in as-cast Al-8.4Zn-1.9Mg-0.85Cu-0.15Zr-xTi alloys
notes: Composition of Ti (x) not specified. Mechanical properties not provided in extracted sections.

key_findings: The addition of Mg forms Mg2Zn11 phase, whose volume fraction increases with Mg content.
The formation of Mg2Zn11 enhances YS and UTS but dramatically deteriorates elongation of hot-extruded Zn-3Cu-(0.1, 0.5, 1.0)Mg alloys compared to hot-extruded Zn-3Cu alloy.
The influence of Mg is stronger in alloys with higher Mg content.
notes: Data extracted from Section 1. The alloy is hot-extruded. Specific numerical values for YS, UTS, and elongation are not provided in the extracted text, only qualitative trends.

paper_title: Microstructure, mechanical properties and in vitro degradation behavior of novel Zn-Cu-Fe alloys
institution: Shanghai Jiao Tong University
key_findings: Zn-3Cu-0.5Fe alloy exhibits good combined mechanical properties with YS 232 MPa, UTS 284 MPa, and elongation 33%.
Addition of Fe increases in vitro degradation rate in SBF by about 52.1% compared to Zn-3Cu alloy.
FeZn13 phase acts as a cathodic phase to the Zn matrix, enhancing micro-galvanic corrosion.
notes: Data extracted from Section 15. Composition calculated as balance Zn (100 - 3 - 0.5 = 96.5 wt%). Corrosion rate is for Zn-3Cu-1Fe alloy (68.9 μm/year), but the mechanical properties listed are specifically for Zn-3Cu-0.5Fe alloy.

key_findings: Wear resistance of ECAP processed Zn–40Al–2Cu–Si material increased due to enhancement in toughness and ductility after ECAP processing.
notes: Alloy composition derived from reference to Purcek et al. [19] in Section 1. Zn content calculated as balance (100 - 40 - 2 - 1 = 57, but standard notation often implies balance to 100; here 58 is used assuming Si is 1 wt%). No other processing or property data provided in the extracted text.

key_findings: Increasing Mg content leads to higher hardness and strength but lower conductivity for Al-Zn-Mg-Cu alloys with constant Zn content (8.5 wt.%), due to higher volume fraction of matrix precipitates.
Increasing Mg content enlarges the area fraction of grain boundary precipitates (GBPs), promoting intergranular fracture and decreasing toughness.
notes: Data extracted from Section 1 of the provided paper content. Only Zn content (8.5 wt.%) is explicitly given; Mg content is described as variable but not numerically specified in the extracted snippet. Other fields are null due to lack of specific numerical data in the evidence.

paper_title: Effect of heat treatments on the microstructure and mechanical properties of Zn-15 wt% Al alloy
institution: Zaozhuang University; Shaoxing Tianlong Tin Materials Co., Ltd
key_findings: Annealing below 275°C results in granular α particles homogeneously distributed in η phase matrix.
Annealing above 275°C followed by air cooling results in lamellar microstructure with isolated η grains.
notes: Data extracted from Section 15. Mechanical properties were not provided in the extracted text.

paper_title: Microstructure, mechanical properties and in vitro degradation properties of Zn–Fe alloys
institution: China University of Petroleum (East China)
key_findings: The FeZn13 phase was observed.
The degradation rate of Zn–2Fe was one order of magnitude higher than that of pure Zn.
The self-corrosion current density of Zn-2Fe was 3.8091 × 10−5 A/cm2.
notes: Data extracted from Section 2 and Section 14. Composition is based on Zn-xFe (x = 0, 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2 wt%). The specific alloy extracted is Zn-2Fe. Mechanical properties (UTS, YS, elongation) are mentioned in figures but numerical values not provided in the text excerpts.

key_findings: After hot-rolling, the grain of Zn-1.5Cu-xAg-0.1Zr alloy was broken and new phase CuZn5 was precipitated.
The mechanical properties were significantly improved after hot-rolling.
notes: Data extracted from Section 3. The alloy is as-rolled Zn-1.5Cu-1.0Ag-0.1Zr. Composition values are in wt%.

key_findings: Hot rolling refines grain size in Zn-Cu-Ti alloys.
Higher Cu and Ti content leads to larger second phases.
Hot-rolled Zn-Cu-Ti alloys have superior corrosion resistance compared to as-cast alloys.
notes: Data extracted from Section 1. Only composition and qualitative findings are available; no numerical mechanical or corrosion properties were provided in the extracted text.

key_findings: Zn-2.0Cu-0.1Ti alloy shows YS of 241.2 MPa, UTS of 265.8 MPa, elongation null30%, Young's modulus 184.9 GPa, hardness 72.9 HV.
notes: Data extracted from Section 12. Composition is in wt%. Hardness in HV. Mechanical properties are for the Zn-2.0Cu-0.1Ti alloy.

key_findings: HR ZnMg-0.2Ce showed highest mechanical property matching among HR samples with moderate UTS, YS, ε, and hardness.
HR ZnMg-0.2Ce showed higher mechanical property and hardness than HR Zn-1Mg alloy.
notes: Data extracted from Section 9. The alloy is hot-rolled (HR). UTS, YS, ε, and hardness values are given as mean ± standard deviation (263±4, 222±9, 14.4±0.8, 90.6±1.0). PSE is null3.8 GPa%.

key_findings: Yield strength of 407 MPa and ultimate tensile strength of 447 MPa were achievable in Al–14Zn–3Mg fabricated in net shape via LPBF, albeit coupled with low ductility (<1.9%).
notes: Data extracted from Section 11. Composition is nominal (Al-14Zn-3Mg). Processing method is LPBF (laser powder bed fusion). Elongation is reported as <1.9%, so 1.9 is used as the upper bound.

key_findings: Addition of 5.5 wt% Al increased the strength of Zn-Al alloy, reaching a maximum tensile strength of 308±8 MPa and yield strength of 240±7 MPa.
notes: Data extracted from Section 2 of the provided text. The paper content mentions Bowen et al. evaluated the mechanical performance of Zn-Al with 5.5 wt% Al. The standard deviation for UTS is ±8 MPa and for YS is ±7 MPa, but only the central values are extracted per instructions.

key_findings: Precipitation strengthening is the dominant strengthening mechanism in Al-Zn-Mg alloys.
The η' phase and Al3M particles contribute to precipitation strengthening via the Orowan mechanism when precipitate size exceeds null2 nm.
The increment in yield strength due to grain boundary strengthening for three alloys with varying Zn/Mg ratio are 60.2 MPa, 58.9 MPa, and 58.2 MPa, respectively.
Ky is 0.43 MPa m1/2.
notes: Data extracted from Section 1 and Section 19. The paper discusses Al-Zn-Mg alloys with varying Zn/Mg ratios. Specific composition values (wt%) are not provided in the extracted text. The yield strength increments from grain boundary strengthening are given, but absolute yield strength values for these specific alloys are not provided in the extracted sections. The ultimate tensile strength and yield strength for four alloys with increasing Zn/Mg ratio are provided in Section 18 (356-394 MPa UTS, 307-342 MPa YS), but these are from a different set of alloys (likely the same study but not explicitly linked to the grain boundary strengthening data in Section 1).

key_findings: Optimum solution treatment is 470°C/60 min.
Ultimate tensile strength reaches 581 MPa, yield strength 561 MPa, elongation 10.1% after aging.
notes: Data extracted from Section 17 and Section 20. The alloy is an Al-Zn-Mg based alloy, but exact composition is not provided in the extracted text. The solution treatment and resulting mechanical properties are clearly stated.

key_findings: Hot rolling refines grain size due to severe plastic deformation and dynamic recrystallization.
Higher Cu and Ti content leads to larger second phases.
Hot-rolled Zn-Cu-Ti alloys have superior corrosion resistance compared to as-cast alloys.
notes: Data extracted from Section 1 and Section 9. Composition is in wt%. Specific mechanical property values for this exact alloy are not provided in the given text.

key_findings: At ambient temperature and 0.0001/s strain rate, the maximum tensile strain of 236.2% was accomplished in Zn-0.5Mn alloy.
With increase in deformation ratio, subgrains are formed and growth limited due to pinning force of MnZn13, resulting in ultra-fine grains.
Ultra-fine grains possess negligible textures, improving ductility by lowering mechanical anisotropy.
notes: Grain size of MnZn13 declined from 0.27 to 0.07 mm against increase in deformation ratio. Data extracted from Section 1.

key_findings: Al–14Zn–3Mg in the AA2 (50 °C/4 h + 150 °C/24 h) condition demonstrated mechanical properties with yield strength of 407 MPa, UTS of 447 MPa albeit with an elongation of only 1.9%.
The main strengthening contribution arises from nano-sized η' and η Mg(Zn, Al, Cu)2 precipitates.
A high volume fraction of η' and η precipitates were present following artificial aging subsequent to solution heat treatment.
The size of η' precipitates ranged between 3 and 7 nm in diameter.
notes: Data extracted from Section 12 and Section 13. The alloy is Al-14Zn-3Mg. The processing condition is AA2 (50 °C/4 h + 150 °C/24 h) after solution heat treatment at 500 °C/4 h. Precipitate size is given as a range (3-7 nm) in Section 13; the value 4 nm is taken from Section 12 where it states 'sizes varying between 4 and 8 nm'.

key_findings: The Zn-600 alloy (Zn-2Sn-0.5Sr treated with 600W ultrasonic power) possessed the smallest capacitive loop and the smallest value of Rct and Rf, implying the best corrosion rate.
Micro galvanic corrosion occurs between primary Zn and SrZn13 phase, with SrZn13 acting as the cathode.
notes: Data extracted from Section 8. The alloy is identified as Zn-600 based on context (ultrasonic power treatment). Composition is nominal from alloy name Zn–2Sn–0.5Sr.

corrosion_properties.corrosion_test_method: potentiodynamic polarization, Tafel extrapolation, immersion test in Hank's solution

key_findings: Zn-1Mn alloy exhibited the highest corrosion rate of 0.3864 mm/year among Zn-xMn alloys tested.
notes: Based on evidence from Section 1. The alloy is Zn-1Mn (wt%). Corrosion rate is from potentiodynamic polarization. Immersion test in Hank's solution for 30 days also performed.

key_findings: Zn-3Cu-0.5Fe alloy exhibits good combined mechanical properties with YS of 232 MPa, UTS of 284 MPa and elongation of 33%.
In vitro degradation rate in SBF was 68.9 ± 7.34 μm/year.
notes: Data extracted from Section 13. The alloy is proposed as a candidate biodegradable material.

microstructure.texture: crystallographic orientation of Zn induced by hot extrusion

key_findings: Hot extrusion leads to almost two-fold increase in UTS and even seven-fold increase in elongation compared to as-cast state.
Dynamic recrystallization during hot extrusion refines grains from 30 μm dendritic branch thickness to 12 μm equiaxed grains.
Eutectic networks disappear after hot extrusion, contributing to strength and plasticity.
Hall-Petch contribution to TYS is 95 MPa, half of measured TYS (203 MPa); remaining strength attributed to Mg-enriched intermetallic phases and crystallographic orientation.
Zn-0.8Mg alloy is promising for biodegradable implants.
notes: Data extracted from Section 1. Grain size of 0.012 mm (12 μm) and TYS of 203 MPa are explicitly stated for the hot-extruded Zn-0.8Mg alloy. Composition is inferred from alloy name.

key_findings: Zn-5WE43 showed the highest tensile strength of 335.4 MPa among the studied compositions.
The elongation of Zn-5WE43 was only 1%.
The compressive strength of Zn-5WE43 porous scaffolds was 73.2 MPa.
The Young's modulus of Zn-5WE43 porous scaffolds was 2480 MPa.
notes: Data extracted from Section 16. The alloy was processed via additive manufacturing (L-PBF) for porous scaffold application. WE43 is a magnesium-based alloy, so the composition is Zn with 5 wt% WE43 addition.

key_findings: Yield strength increases from 307 MPa to 342 MPa as Zn/Mg ratio rises from 3.68 to 6.11.
Precipitation strengthening is the dominant factor influencing yield strength.
Higher Zn/Mg ratio results in narrower PFZ, contributing positively to corrosion resistance.
Highest Zn/Mg ratio of 6.11 exhibits greatest corrosion depth due to continuous grain boundary precipitates.
notes: Data extracted from Section 1 and Section 17. Alloy A (Zn/Mg=3.68) properties at peak aging state. Testing at ambient temperature.

paper_title: Stability of damping capacity and mechanical properties of high-Zn-concentration Al–Zn–Mg–Sc alloy
institution: Key Laboratory of New Processing Technology for Nonferrous Metal & Materials, Ministry of Education, Guilin University of Technology
key_findings: Al–20Zn–0.5Mg–0.5Sc alloy contained fine grains, high number of solution atoms, Zn phases, Al3Sc phases, and high-density fine η and η' phases.
Decreasing Zn content led to disappearance of Zn phases and decrease of intragranular η phases density and solution atom number.
Increasing Mg content did not increase the number of solution atoms.
notes: Data extracted from multiple sections of the paper. Specific numerical mechanical properties for this alloy were not provided in the extracted text. The paper discusses damping capacity and mechanical property stability.

key_findings: The maximum YS and UTS for the ZnMg1.5Ca0.1 alloy were obtained at 160 and 442 MPa, respectively.
The maximum elongation was 5.44% at a Mg content of 1%.
notes: Data extracted from Section 4. The elongation value of 5.44% is for ZnMg1.0Ca0.1, not ZnMg1.5Ca0.1. The UTS and YS values are specifically for the ZnMg1.5Ca0.1 alloy.

key_findings: Two-stage homogenization heat treatment improved T6 hardness, yield strength, and tensile strength compared to one-stage homogenization for both alloy A (0.1Zr) and alloy B (0.05Sc).
Ductility decreased by 5.7% for alloy A and 1.1% for alloy B after two-stage homogenization.
Alloy B (0.05Sc) showed higher hardness, UTS, and YS than alloy A (0.1Zr) under both heat treatment conditions.
notes: Data extracted from Table 3 in Section 1. Mechanical properties reported are for the two-stage homogenization condition (b). Composition is based on alloy designations (0.1Zr and 0.05Sc), but base Al-Zn-Mg composition is not specified in the provided text.

key_findings: Superplastic deformation of Zn-0.033Mg alloy shows strong dependence on deformation temperature.
Ultrafine-grained Zn-0.033Mg alloy achieves superplastic elongation of 376 ± 79% at 453 K and 0.001 s−1.
Grain boundary sliding is the dominant superplastic deformation mechanism, accommodated by {1012} tensile twins.
notes: Elongation value is 376 ± 79% (superplastic elongation). Strain rate is 0.001 s−1. Temperature is 453 K.

key_findings: For as-quenched samples, with increase of Zn/Mg ratio, yield strength increases gradually, ultimate strength increases first then decreases slightly, elongation decreases gradually.
After peak-aging, with increase of Zn/Mg ratio, yield strength increases sharply then remains almost unchanged, tensile strength first increases sharply then slightly, elongation decreases gradually.
The increment of yield strength (ΔRp0.2) after aging increases when Zn/Mg ratio increases from 1.2 to 2.5, then decreases slightly when Zn/Mg ratio further increases to 3.5.
notes: Data extracted from Section 1 of the provided paper content. Only qualitative trends are reported; no specific numerical values for mechanical properties or compositions are given in the evidence. The alloy system is inferred to be Zn-Mg based on the discussion of Zn/Mg ratio.

key_findings: Zn–1.6 Mg alloys reach a maximum UTS of 367 MPa
elongation of near zero proves excessive brittleness
compressive yield strength (CYS) reaches more than 380 MPa
presence of continuous network of hard and brittle Mg2Zn11 intermetallic phases
notes: Data extracted from Section 4. Compressive yield strength is reported as >380 MPa, UTS as 367 MPa, elongation near zero. Mg2Zn11 phase identified.

key_findings: With 0.20 wt% Zr addition, UTS and YS reach maximum values of 296.74 MPa and 249.41 MPa, increases of 26.05% and 33.91% compared to 0 wt% Zr.
Elongation is largest (8.26%) for the alloy with 0.20 wt% Zr.
When Zr content rises to 0.30 wt%, UTS and YS decrease significantly to 197.33 MPa and 160.82 MPa respectively.
notes: Data extracted from Section 3 of the provided content. Composition is based on the reported optimal Zr content of 0.20 wt%. Base metal (Zn) balance is assumed but not explicitly stated.

processing.casting_method: vacuum induction melting, cast into graphite mould, naturally cooled

key_findings: The paper aims to develop 300 MPa grade biodegradable high-strength ductile low-alloy (BHSDLA) Zn-Mn-Mg alloys with EL > 15%.
notes: Nominal composition in wt%. Actual compositions were measured but not provided in the extracted text. Mechanical properties for this specific alloy were not given in the extracted sections.

key_findings: The AC sample exhibited very poor deformation capacity.
notes: Based on Section 8: Zn–5Ce samples were studied. AC (as-cast) sample had very poor deformation capacity; no numerical mechanical properties were provided for the AC state in the extracted text.

paper_title: Effect of heat treatments on the microstructure and mechanical properties of Zn-15 wt% Al alloy
institution: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering, and Materials Science, Zaozhuang University; Shaoxing Tianlong Tin Materials Co., Ltd
key_findings: After being annealed below 275 °C, granular α particles were distributed homogeneously in the η phase matrix.
A lamellar microstructure along with isolated η grains was observed when the alloys were annealed above 275 °C, followed by air cooling.
Tensile strength for air-cooled samples is little changed with aging time, but sharply decreases for water-quenched samples.
notes: Data extracted from Section 9 and Section 11. Specific mechanical property values (UTS, YS, EL) for this alloy are not provided in the extracted sections; only qualitative trends are described.

key_findings: T6I4 treatment results in elongated η phases and clean grain boundaries without noticeable precipitates.
T6I4 treatment yields the highest elongation (2.5%) among T4, T6, and T6I4 conditions.
T6I4 treatment shifts open-circuit potential to -0.74 V.
notes: Data extracted from Section 1 and Section 13. Composition not specified in provided text. Corrosion potential from T6I4 treatment.

key_findings: ZS-XAl alloys with Al addition (0-0.09 wt%) show UTS ranging from 67.28 to 78.61 MPa and elongation from 32.83% to 41.41% at room temperature with strain rate 8.3e-4 s-1.
Al addition enhances undercooling and promotes nucleation in Zn-25Sn-XAl alloys.
Long-term reliability of Zn-25Sn-XAl alloy may need further investigation due to Al segregation at grain boundaries.
notes: Data extracted from Section 1 and Section 15. Composition: Zn-25Sn-XAl with X=0-0.09 wt% Al. The UTS and elongation ranges given are for the entire X range (0-0.09 wt% Al). Maximum values reported here.

key_findings: The paper discusses the effect of Zn/Mg ratio on microstructure, mechanical properties, and corrosion resistance of Al-Zn-Mg alloys.
With decreasing Zn/Mg ratio from 11.4 to 6.1, grain boundary precipitates (GBPs) become coarser and discontinuously distributed.
Tensile strength increased by 34.1% and yield strength by 47.4% when Zn/Mg ratio decreased from 11.4 to 6.1.
Stress corrosion sensitivity index decreased from 0.031 to 0.007 with decreasing Zn/Mg ratio.
Strengthening mechanisms include solid-solution, grain-boundary, and precipitation strengthening.
notes: Based on limited evidence from Section 1 and Section 12. Specific composition values (wt%) are not provided in the extracted text; only Zn/Mg ratios (11.4, 8.1, 6.1) are given. Mechanical properties for three alloys with these ratios are reported: UTS 333.2, 407.3, 446.9 MPa; YS 269.4, 352.9, 397.2 MPa; Elongation 18.4%, 17.6%, 18.9% respectively. These values are not inserted into the structured fields because the alloy_id is generic and the composition is incomplete.

key_findings: Cold hydrostatic extrusion (HE) proposed to enhance mechanical properties of Zn-1%Mg alloy.
HE increases plasticity, allowing deformation at room temperature.
High extrusion speed promotes grain refinement and improvement in mechanical properties.
notes: Based on Section 3. Alloy composition is Zn with 1% wt. magnesium. Annealing at 300°C for 24h followed by water quench. Paper title and full author list not provided in the excerpt.

key_findings: Both ultimate tensile strength and elongation of Zn-Mn alloys increase due to minor Mn additions.
With increasing Mn content from 0.2 to 0.6 wt%, the ultimate tensile strength and yield strength of as-extruded Zn-Mn alloys change.
notes: The paper content provides qualitative trends for Zn-Mn alloys with 0.2-0.6 wt% Mn, but exact numerical values for mechanical properties at specific Mn contents are not extractable from the provided text. The as-extruded pure Zn reference values are UTS=117 MPa, elongation=14%.

paper_title: Effect of Zn alloying on the mechanical and damping properties of Cu-Al-Zn alloys
institution: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Southwest Jiaotong University
key_findings: Cu-Al-1Zn alloy achieved the highest damping ratio (0.0172) at high frequencies.
Zn addition increases γ2 phase volume fraction and interface density, enhancing energy dissipation capacity.
At high frequencies, damping ratio first increases then decreases with rising Zn content due to competition between interface gain and material embrittlement.
notes: Damping ratio (tanδ) of 0.0172 reported for Cu-Al-1Zn alloy. Data extracted from Sections 2 and 7 of the provided content.

key_findings: Multistage aging treatment increases UTS to nullimately 409 MPa, but decreases elongation to 0.6–1.6%.
Solution and aging treatment decreases UTS to 341–349 MPa but increases elongation to 17.6%.
notes: Data extracted from Section 17. Composition not provided in the given text. UTS and elongation values are for the multistage aging condition (highest UTS).

key_findings: Zn–1Mg–0.1Er/Dy/Ho ternary alloys were prepared by casting.
Hot-rolling was carried out to enhance mechanical properties.
Mechanical properties, corrosion behavior, hemolysis rate, APTT, and cytotoxicity were evaluated.
notes: Composition is nominal in wt%. Zn content calculated as balance (100 - 1 - 0.1 = 98.9). Paper title, DOI, authors, and institution not provided in the extracted sections.

key_findings: With 0.20 wt% Zr addition, UTS reaches 296.74 MPa and YS reaches 249.41 MPa, increases of 26.05% and 33.91% compared to 0 wt% Zr.
Elongation is 8.26% for the alloy with 0.20 wt% Zr.
UTS and YS first increase then decrease with increasing Zr content; at 0.30 wt% Zr, UTS drops to 197.33 MPa and YS to 160.82 MPa.
notes: Data extracted from Section 4 of the provided text. Only the Zn-0.20Zr alloy composition and its mechanical properties are explicitly reported with numerical values. Other fields are null due to lack of evidence.

key_findings: After 1 year storage at 25°C, tensile stress increased to 185.7 MPa and elongation decreased to 11.3%.
notes: Data extracted from Section 14, Table 2. Aging time converted from 1 year to hours. Yield strength not explicitly provided; tensile stress reported as 'Tensile Stress (MPa)' which is interpreted as UTS.

paper_title: Biodegradable Zn-Mg-Ce alloys with good mechanical properties, degradability, and cytocompatibility matching for potential load-bearing bone-implant applications
institution: Wenzhou Medical University
key_findings: Zn alloys are expected to be a potential biodegradable bone implant material due to biodegradability, good biological effect, and easy formability.
Lower mechanical properties and low degradation rate are challenges for Zn alloys.
notes: Based on limited evidence from Section 3 only; the paper is an abstract/introduction and does not provide specific numerical data for the Zn-Mg-Ce alloy composition, processing, or properties.

key_findings: Alloy A had the lowest hardness among the four alloys studied.
Peak hardness of 112.8 HV was achieved after aging at 120°C for 24 hours.
Enhancing the Zn/Mg ratio effectively boosts the alloy's peak hardness.
notes: Data extracted from Section 3. Alloy A is one of four Al-Zn-Mg alloys (A, B, C, D) with varying Zn/Mg ratios. Composition details not provided in the extracted text.

key_findings: Addition of 5.5 wt% Al to Zn increased strength, reaching a maximum tensile strength of 308±8 MPa and yield strength of 240±7 MPa.
notes: Data extracted from Section 1, referencing Bowen et al. [18]. The UTS and YS values are reported with error margins (±8 MPa and ±7 MPa respectively). Only the numerical central values are extracted per instructions.

key_findings: At ambient temperature and 0.0001/s strain rate, maximum tensile strain of 236.2% was accomplished in Zn-0.5Mn alloy.
Grain size of MnZn13 declined from 0.27 to 0.07 mm with increase in deformation ratio.
Ultra-fine grains with negligible textures improve ductility by lowering mechanical anisotropy.
notes: Data extracted from Section 1. Grain size of MnZn13 phase reported in mm, not the Zn matrix. Strain rate is 0.0001 s^-1.

key_findings: As-extruded Zn-1.2Mg alloy showed higher yield strength, ultimate tensile strength, and hardness than pure zinc.
Corrosion rate of as-extruded alloy was higher than as-cast alloy, reaching 0.19 mm/year in electrochemical tests.
notes: Data extracted from Section 2 and Section 8. The alloy was fabricated by extrusion. The corrosion rate from immersion in Hank's solution for 30 days was 0.11 mm/year.

key_findings: ZSCB solder has tensile strength of 125.24 MPa and elongation of 0.71%.
Coupling modification (RE + rapid cooling) increased tensile strength to 221.86 MPa and elongation to 1.51%.
Microstructure consists of dendritic ε phase in η matrix, with β-Sn and Bi at grain boundaries.
notes: Based on evidence from Sections 1, 2, 4, and 14. Composition calculated as balance Zn (100 - 5 - 2 - 1.5 = 91.5 wt%). Year inferred from copyright 2016 Elsevier.

paper_title: Microstructure refinement, mechanical performances and degradability of biomedical Zn-2Cu alloy by ultrasonic melting and homogenization treatment

processing.casting_method: ultrasonic melt treatment (UMT) with different ultrasonic powers (0 W, 300 W, 600 W, and 900 W)

key_findings: Under UMT, coarse α-Zn grains of Zn-2Cu alloy were refined into fine equiaxed grains.

Precipitating CuZn5 phase was significantly refined from coarse block into fine structure.
